passages.title: The Inheritance
passages.text:
The morning Mr. Harcourt read his uncle's will aloud, the parlour was so still that the ticking of the mantel-clock seemed an impertinence. Clara sat with her hands folded, watching the faces of her cousins rather than the solicitor. She had learned long ago that a legacy is revealed not in the reading of a document but in the twitch of a lip, the sudden interest of an eye.

"To my niece Clara," the solicitor pronounced, "I leave my library, and the sum of one hundred pounds."

A hundred pounds. It was, she knew, a trifle beside the estate that now passed to her cousin Edmund; and yet she felt the eyes of the room settle upon her with a new and curious weight. Edmund, who a moment before had been all warmth, now inclined his head with the gravity of a man who has come into property and means to be reminded of it.

"The library," murmured Aunt Prudence, "is a great many books to dust."

Clara said nothing. She was thinking of the winter evenings when her uncle, half-blind, had asked her to read to him — Herodotus, and the sermons of Dr. Tillotson, and once, scandalously, a French novel he had smuggled past the disapproval of the household. He had never spoken to her of gratitude. But he had left her the room in which they had been, for those hours, entirely at ease with one another; and he had left it to her alone.

"You will sell them, of course," said Edmund. It was not quite a question.

"I think not," said Clara.

Something passed across his face — surprise, then a calculation she could not read. Outside, a carriage was brought round; the mourners would soon disperse to their separate houses and their separate opinions of her. Clara rose and crossed to the window. A hundred pounds would not keep her a twelvemonth. But she found, to her own faint astonishment, that she was not afraid.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: a legal dispute among relatives over an estate
Clara's private recognition that a modest bequest carries a value the others cannot see
Edmund's determination to cheat Clara of her inheritance
the social customs governing a Victorian household in mourning
Clara's growing terror at the prospect of poverty
questions.explanation: Clara values the library for the hours it represents, a worth invisible to others. (A) and (C) overstate conflict never shown; (D) is background, not the point; (E) is contradicted by her closing calm, 'she was not afraid.'

questions.prompt: When the library is first mentioned, Aunt Prudence responds by
choices: offering to help Clara care for the books
observing that it is a great many books to dust
urging Clara to sell the collection at once
reminding the room of the uncle's blindness
leaving the parlour in tears
questions.explanation: The text gives Aunt Prudence the line about dusting. Selling (C) is Edmund's suggestion, not hers; (A), (D), and (E) never occur in the passage.

questions.prompt: It can most reasonably be inferred that Edmund's manner cools after the will is read because
choices: he has secretly always disliked Clara
his sudden wealth has made him newly conscious of his own standing
he fears Clara intends to challenge the will in court
he is overcome with grief for his uncle
he is ashamed of wanting the library for himself
questions.explanation: He inclines his head 'like a man who has come into property,' signaling status-consciousness. (C) invents a legal threat; (A) is too extreme and unsupported; (D) contradicts his calculating look; (E) misreads his interest.

questions.prompt: In the second paragraph, 'trifle' most nearly means
choices: a light amusement
a thing of little worth
a sweet dessert
a deliberate insult
a heavy burden
questions.explanation: A hundred pounds is small 'beside the estate,' so trifle means something of little value. (C) and (A) are dictionary senses wrong here; (D) and (E) import a weight the sentence does not carry.

questions.prompt: Clara's state of mind in the final paragraph is best described as
choices: triumphant and eager for revenge
quietly resolved
overwhelmed by despair
wholly indifferent to her future
bitterly resentful of her cousins
questions.explanation: She keeps the books and finds she 'was not afraid' — a calm, settled resolve. (C) and (E) overstate negative feeling; (A) overstates aggression; (D) is wrong because she plainly cares about the library and her position.

questions.prompt: The passage indicates that Clara read to her uncle from all of the following EXCEPT
choices: Herodotus
the sermons of Dr. Tillotson
a French novel
the poetry of Milton
a book he had smuggled past the household
questions.explanation: Herodotus, the sermons, and the smuggled French novel are all named; Milton is not. (E) simply restates the French novel's description, so it is supported.

passages.title: Signal
passages.text:
Maya's father had been texting her the weather. Not asking about her weather — reporting his. "34 and clear here," he'd write from two thousand miles away, as though she'd requested a forecast for a city she had left at eighteen and never planned to return to.

She used to find it maddening. For years she had wanted him to ask questions: about her job, about the apartment, about whether she was happy. Instead he sent barometric pressure and the occasional photograph of the backyard maple, captioned only with the date.

Now, waiting in the airport for the flight home, she scrolled back through two years of these messages. "42, rain." "Snow overnight, 6 inches." "Maple turning." She had answered most of them with a thumbs-up, the small gray icon that meant received and required nothing further of her.

It was her sister who had called about the diagnosis. Her father would not have mentioned it; he would have gone on sending weather until he couldn't. Maya understood this now the way you understand a language you were raised in but never learned to speak — imperfectly, and all at once.

She typed: "What's it like there today?" and then deleted it. It was the wrong question, or the right question asked too late. She put the phone in her pocket.

When she landed, there was a message waiting. "Cold front coming through. You'll want a jacket." She stood on the jet bridge, travelers pressing past her, and read it three times. He had checked the weather where she was going. He had, in the only vocabulary he owned, been asking after her all along.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: A father and daughter reconcile after a long estrangement
A daughter comes to recognize her father's indirect way of showing that he cares
A family struggles to cope with a sudden medical diagnosis
A woman regrets leaving her hometown at eighteen
A father learns to ask his daughter better questions
questions.explanation: Maya realizes the weather texts were affection 'in the only vocabulary he owned.' (C) and (E) are peripheral; (A) overstates estrangement; (D) is a passing detail, not the point.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, Maya usually replied to her father's messages with
choices: a detailed account of her day
a thumbs-up icon
a question about his health
a photograph of her apartment
no reply at all
questions.explanation: The text says she answered most with 'a thumbs-up... that meant received and required nothing further.' (E) is close but wrong — she did reply; the others never appear.

questions.prompt: The passage most strongly suggests that Maya's father sends weather reports because
choices: he is unaware that Maya finds them irritating
reporting the weather is his available way of reaching out
he wants Maya to worry about conditions back home
he has nothing else to talk about
he is testing whether Maya will finally call him
questions.explanation: The closing lines reveal he'd 'been asking after her all along' in his only vocabulary. (D) is too dismissive; (C) and (E) invent motives; (A) misses that the point is his manner of caring, not his awareness.

questions.prompt: In the final paragraph, 'vocabulary' most nearly means
choices: a list of defined words
an available means of expression
a body of knowledge about weather
a foreign language
a particular tone of voice
questions.explanation: 'The only vocabulary he owned' names the sole way he can express feeling. (A) is the literal sense, wrong here; (C), (D), and (E) each seize on one association without fitting the metaphor.

questions.prompt: Maya's attitude toward her father's messages shifts over the passage from
choices: amusement to boredom
exasperation to tender understanding
indifference to anger
gratitude to disappointment
fear to relief
questions.explanation: She once found them 'maddening' but ends reading his message three times, moved. (D) and (E) reverse the arc; (A) and (C) miss the warmth she arrives at.

questions.prompt: The father's final message — 'Cold front coming through. You'll want a jacket.' — chiefly functions to
choices: warn Maya about dangerous travel conditions
show that his habitual gesture is, at last, aimed directly at her
prove that he has changed his behavior since the diagnosis
reveal that he knows nothing of her real life
provide comic relief before a sad ending
questions.explanation: He checked the weather where she was landing — the same gesture, now clearly about her. (C) is wrong; it's the same habit, newly understood. (A), (D), and (E) miss the emotional revelation.

passages.title: The Uses of Forgetting
passages.text:
We tend to treat forgetting as a failure — a leak in the bucket of memory, an enemy to be defeated with flashcards and mnemonic tricks. But a growing body of research suggests that forgetting is not the breakdown of a well-designed system; it is part of the design.

Consider what a memory would be worth if it retained everything. A brain that stored every license plate, every overheard conversation, every route not taken would drown in its own records. To recall your current phone number, you would have to sort past every number you had ever held. The value of memory lies not in hoarding but in selection — and selection means letting most of it go.

Neuroscientists now describe forgetting as an active process, not merely passive decay. The brain appears to run something like a background editor, weakening connections that have gone unused and preserving those that recur or carry emotional weight. This is why you remember the morning of an accident in vivid detail and forget a thousand ordinary breakfasts. The system is not malfunctioning when it discards the breakfasts. It is doing its job.

There is a cost, of course. The same process that clears away clutter can carry off things we wish we had kept, and it can be hijacked — by trauma, by age, by disease. But to define forgetting solely by its failures is like judging a river only by its floods. Under ordinary conditions, forgetting keeps memory usable, allowing generalization: we extract the rule and shed the thousand particular instances that taught it.

The student who forgets the exact wording of a definition but retains its meaning has not lost anything essential. She has done what the mind is built to do. Perhaps the goal of a good memory is not to forget less, but to forget well.

questions.prompt: The central claim of the passage is that
choices: forgetting is a flaw that better study habits can overcome
forgetting is an active, adaptive feature of how memory is designed
the brain stores every experience but cannot always retrieve it
emotional memories are more accurate than ordinary ones
memory inevitably declines with age and disease
questions.explanation: The author argues forgetting 'is part of the design,' an active editor that keeps memory usable. (A) states the view he rejects; (C) contradicts the text; (D) and (E) are details, not the thesis.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the brain tends to preserve memories that
choices: involve license plates and phone numbers
recur or carry emotional weight
were formed most recently
the person consciously chooses to keep
occur during ordinary routines
questions.explanation: The 'background editor' preserves connections that 'recur or carry emotional weight.' (E) is the opposite — routine breakfasts are discarded; (A), (C), and (D) are not the stated criteria.

questions.prompt: The author would most likely agree that a memory retaining absolutely everything would be
choices: the ideal toward which the brain evolves
a burden that made ordinary recall harder
possible only in people with unusual talent
free of the costs the author describes
identical to how memory already works
questions.explanation: Such a brain 'would drown in its own records,' unable to find a phone number. (A) reverses the argument; (D) ignores the drowning cost; (C) and (E) are unsupported.

questions.prompt: In the second paragraph, 'hoarding' most nearly means
choices: storing without discarding
organizing carefully
sharing freely
hiding from others
forgetting gradually
questions.explanation: 'Hoarding' is opposed to 'selection' — amassing everything rather than letting go. (C) and (B) miss the excess; (D) adds secrecy not implied; (E) is the opposite of retaining.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward forgetting is best described as
choices: alarmed and cautionary
appreciative and reappraising
coldly neutral and uninvolved
nostalgic and mournful
dismissive of any drawbacks
questions.explanation: He reframes forgetting as well-designed while conceding costs — an appreciative reappraisal. (E) is wrong because he acknowledges trauma and disease; (A) overstates worry; (C) ignores his clear stance; (D) is off-key.

questions.prompt: The comparison of forgetting to 'a river only by its floods' primarily serves to
choices: warn that forgetting is dangerous like a flood
argue that judging forgetting by its failures ignores its everyday value
prove that memory and water behave in the same way
suggest that forgetting cannot be controlled
illustrate how quickly memories disappear
questions.explanation: A river isn't defined by rare floods, just as forgetting shouldn't be defined by its failures. (A) inverts the point; (C) takes the analogy too literally; (D) and (E) address ideas the image isn't making.

passages.title: Clocks for the Railroad
passages.text:
Before the 1880s, time in most towns was a local affair. Noon was the moment the sun stood highest over that particular place, and a clockmaker or a church bell set the hour accordingly. A traveler going west found that his watch, faithfully accurate in Boston, grew steadily wrong with every mile. There were, by one count, more than three hundred local times in North America, each of them correct where it stood.

The railroads changed this, and it is worth being clear about why. Trains did not require standard time because standard time was true; they required it because a schedule cannot be printed for three hundred different noons. A single minute of confusion between two stations, on a single track, could put two trains in the same place at the same moment. Standard time was, first of all, a safety measure and an accounting convenience — a way of making the map legible to the timetable.

In 1883 the railroad companies, not the government, divided the continent into zones and declared, in effect, when noon would occur. Cities adjusted their clocks to match the trains. Some resisted: a few towns kept "sun time" for years, insisting that no railroad could tell them what hour it was. But the logic of the network was stronger than local pride, and eventually the law caught up to what the companies had already done.

We often tell this story as simple progress — chaos giving way to order. And in one sense it was. But it is worth noticing what was traded. A town that once kept its own noon now kept a noon dictated by a distant office, useful to everyone and belonging to no one. Standardization gave us the possibility of a shared schedule; it also taught us to accept that the authoritative version of something as intimate as the hour might be set elsewhere, by people we would never meet.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: proving that standard time is scientifically more accurate than local time
how the shift to standard time solved railroad problems while relocating authority over daily life
celebrating the railroads for bringing order out of chaos
the technical difficulty of building a transcontinental railroad
a dispute between the government and the railroads over who set the clocks
questions.explanation: The essay explains the practical need and then what was 'traded' — authority over the hour. (C) ignores the critical turn; (A) is contradicted ('not because it was true'); (D) and (E) are not the focus.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the division of the continent into time zones in 1883 was carried out by
choices: the federal government
the railroad companies
town clockmakers
church authorities
an international scientific committee
questions.explanation: The text states 'the railroad companies, not the government' declared the zones. (A) is the explicit contrast; (C), (D), and (E) are not credited with the change.

questions.prompt: The passage suggests that towns eventually abandoned 'sun time' mainly because
choices: they became convinced sun time was inaccurate
the demands of the rail network outweighed local independence
the government made local time illegal immediately
clockmakers refused to keep setting local hours
travelers complained about confusing schedules
questions.explanation: 'The logic of the network was stronger than local pride.' (A) is wrong — accuracy wasn't the reason; (C) reverses the order (law came after); (D) and (E) are not given as the cause.

questions.prompt: In the second paragraph, 'legible' most nearly means
choices: written in large print
able to be read and made sense of
profitable
permanent
attractive to travelers
questions.explanation: Making the map 'legible to the timetable' means intelligible and coordinatable. (A) is the narrow literal sense about handwriting; (C), (D), and (E) import meanings the word does not carry here.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward the standardization of time is best described as
choices: unreservedly celebratory
measured, weighing a real gain against a real cost
sharply condemning
confused and undecided
indifferent to its effects
questions.explanation: He grants the safety benefit yet notes what was lost — a balanced appraisal. (A) and (C) pick one side only; (D) mistakes balance for confusion; (E) ignores his evident engagement.

questions.prompt: The final paragraph chiefly serves to
choices: summarize the technical steps of adopting standard time
complicate the 'simple progress' story by naming what standardization cost
praise the railroads for their foresight
predict that local time will someday return
explain how time zones function today
questions.explanation: It questions the 'chaos giving way to order' narrative by noting a noon 'belonging to no one.' (C) misreads the tone; (A), (D), and (E) describe things the paragraph does not do.

passages.title: Quarters
passages.text:
My grandmother ran a laundromat, and for three summers I ran it with her. This is a less romantic sentence than it sounds. A laundromat is a room that is always either too hot or too loud, and usually both, and the smell of dryer sheets gets into your hair so completely that friends could tell where I had spent my day before I said a word.

My job was quarters. I made change, I coaxed the bill-changer when it jammed, and I mopped. My grandmother's job was everything else, though I did not understand at the time how much "everything else" contained. To me she seemed simply to sit near the window, greeting the regulars, occasionally rising to thump a machine that had eaten someone's money.

What I could not see was that she was the reason they came. There was a cheaper laundromat two blocks over, brighter and newer, with machines that took cards. Our regulars walked past it to reach us. I assumed this was loyalty, or habit. It was years before I understood it was her: that she remembered which woman had a son overseas, and asked after him; that she let a man short on rent run a tab in a business that had no tabs; that she had, without ever using the word, made a public room feel like a kept one.

The summer I turned sixteen I told her I thought we should get card machines. She listened, the way she listened to everyone, and said maybe someday. We never did. I was faintly embarrassed by this at the time, by the jammed changer and the mop and the quarters that left my hands smelling of metal.

I am not embarrassed now. I understand that she was not behind the times. She had simply decided which things she would not trade for convenience, and she was right about all of them.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily about
choices: the unpleasant physical conditions of working in a laundromat
how the narrator came to understand and admire the grandmother's values
the financial decline of a family business
the rivalry between two neighborhood laundromats
the narrator's plan to modernize the business with card machines
questions.explanation: The narrator moves from embarrassment to seeing the grandmother 'was right about all of them.' (A) and (E) are details; (C) never happens; (D) is background that highlights her, not the subject.

questions.prompt: The narrator's own duties at the laundromat included
choices: greeting the regular customers
making change and mopping
keeping the business's accounts
deciding which customers could run a tab
repairing the machines
questions.explanation: 'My job was quarters' — making change and mopping. Greeting regulars and extending credit were the grandmother's 'everything else'; (C) and (E) are not the narrator's tasks.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that the regulars walked past the newer laundromat because
choices: its machines were unreliable
of the grandmother's personal attention to them
it was more expensive than the grandmother's
they disliked using card machines
it was farther from their homes
questions.explanation: The narrator learns 'it was her' — she remembered their lives and made the room feel 'kept.' (C) reverses the facts (the other was cheaper); (A), (D), and (E) are never suggested.

questions.prompt: In the second paragraph, 'coaxed' most nearly means
choices: forced open by hand
patiently worked into functioning
permanently repaired
unplugged and reset
replaced with a new one
questions.explanation: She 'coaxed the bill-changer when it jammed' — gently got it working again. (A) adds force the word resists; (C), (D), and (E) describe fixes the text does not mention.

questions.prompt: Looking back, the narrator regards the grandmother with
choices: lingering embarrassment
respect and admiration
mild amusement
guarded resentment
detached curiosity
questions.explanation: 'I am not embarrassed now... she was right about all of them.' (A) is the earlier feeling the narrator outgrows; (D) and (E) miss the warmth; (C) trivializes the mature respect.

questions.prompt: The mention of the cheaper laundromat 'two blocks over' primarily serves to
choices: explain why the family business eventually failed
underscore that the regulars chose the grandmother deliberately, not for convenience
criticize customers who valued newer machines
show that the narrator's modernization idea was sensible
describe the layout of the neighborhood
questions.explanation: A closer, cheaper, newer rival existed, yet regulars walked past it — proving they came for her. (A) assumes a failure never stated; (D) and (C) miss the point; (E) treats a meaningful detail as mere setting.

passages.title: Low Tide
passages.text:
All morning we had gathered what the sea let go —
the green glass worn to candy, the split shells,
the crab-back light as paper, the rope
still knotted to nothing. My brother filled
his pockets till they sagged, sure
that a thing found is a thing kept.

The tide came in the way that all tides come,
not as a wave but as a rumor, then a fact.
We watched the flat we'd crossed become a bay.
Our footprints went first, then the low rocks,
then the place where we had knelt.

He cried a little, for the map of it,
the proof that we had been there, going under.
I did not tell him what I have since learned:
that the sea keeps nothing either,
and that this is not the same as loss —
that some things are ours precisely
because we cannot hold them.

questions.prompt: The poem primarily suggests that
choices: collecting objects from the beach is a waste of effort
the impermanence of things need not be experienced as loss
the sea is a cruel and destructive force
children cannot understand the natural world
memories fade more quickly than physical objects
questions.explanation: The close insists impermanence 'is not the same as loss' — some things are ours because we can't hold them. (C) and (A) are too negative; (D) is condescending and unsupported; (E) misstates the poem's point.

questions.prompt: In the poem, the brother responds to the objects he finds by
choices: throwing them back into the sea
filling his pockets until they sag
giving them to the speaker
arranging them on the low rocks
counting them carefully
questions.explanation: 'My brother filled his pockets till they sagged.' The other actions do not appear; (A) even contradicts his wish to keep what he finds.

questions.prompt: The line 'a thing found is a thing kept' reflects the brother's belief that
choices: found objects legally belong to whoever finds them
what one gathers can be held onto permanently
the sea will always provide more treasures
sharing his finds would diminish them
the tide will not reach their collection
questions.explanation: The brother assumes possession is permanent — a view the vanishing tide overturns. (E) is a related but narrower misread; (A), (C), and (D) introduce ideas the line does not express.

questions.prompt: As used in the poem, 'rumor' (the tide came 'not as a wave but as a rumor, then a fact') most nearly means
choices: a loud, crashing arrival
a faint, gradual approach sensed before it is seen
a false or untrue story
a sudden and violent surge
a spoken warning from the speaker
questions.explanation: The tide arrives quietly, by hint, before becoming undeniable ('then a fact'). (A) and (D) describe the 'wave' the poem denies; (C) is the everyday sense; (E) invents speech not present.

questions.prompt: The tone of the poem's final lines is best described as
choices: bitter and defeated
calm and accepting
anxious and uncertain
playful and teasing
angry at the sea
questions.explanation: The speaker calmly reframes loss as a kind of belonging. (A) and (E) are too negative; (C) misses the settled understanding; (D) mistakes the quiet, serious close for lightness.

questions.prompt: The contrast between the brother and the speaker chiefly serves to
choices: show that the speaker is cleverer and enjoys proving it
set a child's wish to possess against the speaker's acceptance of impermanence
explain the scientific cause of ocean tides
criticize the brother for crying at the beach
describe the appearance of the shoreline in detail
questions.explanation: The brother clings to 'proof'; the speaker has learned to let go — the poem's central contrast. (A) misreads the gentle tone; (D) is unkind and unsupported; (C) and (E) name functions the contrast doesn't serve.

passages.title: The Stopped Clocks
passages.text:
The carriage left Miss Ellery at the gates of Harrowfield as the last daylight thinned behind the elms. No servant came to meet her; only a boy of ten or so stood upon the steps, regarding her with the grave, unhurried attention of a much older person. She had expected to be received with some ceremony, for the letter engaging her had spoken warmly of the family's eagerness. Now the warmth of that letter seemed to belong to another house entirely.

"You are the new governess," the boy said. It was not a question. "Mother is not down. She is seldom down before noon, and it is nearly six."

Miss Ellery, who had traveled since dawn, set her single trunk upon the gravel and considered him. "Then perhaps you might show me in yourself. I find I am not too proud to be received by a gentleman of your years."

Something flickered across his face—surprise, and beneath it, so quickly hidden she nearly missed it, a flash of relief. He had expected her to scold, she thought, or to weep, or to demand things the house could not give. Instead she had made a small joke and trusted him with it. He turned without another word and led her through a hall where the mirrors were draped and the clocks, every one of them, had been stopped at the same hour.

She did not ask why. She had learned, in a life of entering other people's houses, that a household guards its silences as jealously as its silver, and that the surest way to be turned out was to reach for a door before it was offered. She would wait. The clocks would keep their secret a while longer, and so, for now, would she.

questions.prompt: The passage primarily serves to
choices: reveal that Miss Ellery regrets accepting the position
present a first meeting that hints at hidden trouble in the household
portray the boy's resentment of his new governess
describe the daily routine of a wealthy family
dramatize a quarrel between Miss Ellery and the boy's mother
questions.explanation: B: the scene builds quiet unease—draped mirrors, stopped clocks, an absent mother. A overstates; she shows no regret. C reverses the boy's actual relief.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, Miss Ellery is surprised by her reception because
choices: the boy refuses to speak to her
the letter that hired her had promised an eager welcome
the carriage arrives earlier than expected
no rooms have been prepared for her
the mother scolds her at the door
questions.explanation: B: the letter 'spoke warmly of the family's eagerness,' clashing with her cold reception. The other options describe events the passage never states.

questions.prompt: The boy's "flash of relief" most strongly suggests that he
choices: had feared the new governess would be harsh or difficult
secretly disliked his mother
hoped to be sent away to school
recognized Miss Ellery from an earlier meeting
was glad the carriage had finally gone
questions.explanation: A: he had braced for scolding or tears; her small joke disarms him. B–E invent motives the text does not support.

questions.prompt: In context, "guards its silences as jealously as its silver," the word "jealously" most nearly means
choices: enviously
suspiciously
protectively
angrily
secretly
questions.explanation: C: to guard jealously is to protect vigilantly. E, 'secretly,' names what is hidden, not the manner in which the house guards it.

questions.prompt: Miss Ellery's attitude toward the household's mysteries is best described as
choices: frightened and eager to leave
patient and observant
indifferent and bored
suspicious and accusing
cheerful and untroubled
questions.explanation: B: she waits, watches, and withholds her questions. E is half-right—she is calm but not 'untroubled,' since the stopped clocks unsettle the scene.

questions.prompt: The detail that the clocks were "stopped at the same hour" serves mainly to
choices: suggest that some past event haunts the house
show that the family has become poor
explain why the mother sleeps late
prove that the boy is lying
indicate how late Miss Ellery has arrived
questions.explanation: A: identical stopped clocks hint at a fixed, haunting past. E is true (it is evening), but the frozen clocks are not about her arrival time.

passages.title: Two, Please
passages.text:
By the third week, Nadia could tell the regulars apart by their shoes before she ever looked up. The man in the scuffed loafers wanted extra cilantro and no ice. The woman in white sneakers always paid with exact change and never said thank you, though she came back every day, which Nadia had decided counted for more. From inside the cart, the world was mostly feet and hands and the small square of counter where money crossed over.

Her father hummed while he worked. He had hummed the same three songs since Nadia was small, back when he wore a badge and a lanyard and came home talking about load calculations she didn't understand. He did not talk about load calculations anymore. He talked about limes—which vendor cheated on weight, which stall's cilantro wilted by noon—and he talked about it with the same seriousness, as though the fate of a bridge depended on it.

At school she had told no one what her father did now. When Priya asked, Nadia had said "he's between things," which was true in the way that a person falling is between the roof and the ground. But standing in the cart, watching him fold a tortilla with the exact patience he must once have brought to a blueprint, she felt the lie curdle in her.

A boy from her chemistry class stepped up to order. Nadia braced for the flicker of recognition, the story that would travel back down the hallways. He looked right at her.

"Two, please," he said, and dug for his wallet, and that was all. He did not know her. Or he knew her and did not care, which she was beginning to suspect might be the same, ordinary mercy.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: Nadia's growing understanding of her father and her own shame
the difficulty of running a food cart profitably
a budding romance between Nadia and a classmate
a father teaching his daughter how to cook
Nadia's plan to quit working for her father
questions.explanation: A: Nadia watches her father with new tenderness while wrestling with her own shame. The other choices fix on details or events the passage does not center on.

questions.prompt: Before losing his job, Nadia's father most likely worked as
choices: a chef
a market vendor
an engineer
a schoolteacher
a bridge inspector
questions.explanation: C: the badge, lanyard, 'load calculations,' and 'blueprint' mark him an engineer. E is a trap—'bridge' appears only in a simile about limes.

questions.prompt: In context, "she felt the lie curdle in her," the word "curdle" most nearly means
choices: disappear
turn sour and spoil
grow louder
freeze solid
harden into resolve
questions.explanation: B: to curdle is to sour and spoil, matching her souring guilt. E, 'harden into resolve,' contradicts the queasy discomfort the moment describes.

questions.prompt: Nadia's judgment that the woman's daily return "counted for more" than a thank-you suggests that she
choices: resents customers who do not tip
values steady loyalty over polite words
wishes the woman would stop coming
is angry at her father's customers
hopes to become the woman's friend
questions.explanation: B: showing up daily outweighs a missing thank-you—loyalty over politeness. A and D read an anger the passage never shows.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward the father is best described as
choices: mocking and dismissive
tender and quietly admiring
cold and indifferent
resentful and bitter
pitying and ashamed
questions.explanation: B: the father is drawn with dignity ('the exact patience'). E is half-right—the shame belongs to Nadia's lie, not to how she regards him.

questions.prompt: The final paragraph, in which the classmate simply orders "Two, please," chiefly serves to
choices: offer Nadia an unexpected relief from her dread
reveal that the boy is unkind
show that Nadia's secret has been exposed
introduce a new romantic subplot
prove that the cart's food is popular
questions.explanation: A: the boy's indifference becomes an 'ordinary mercy' that lifts her dread. C reverses it—her secret is not exposed at all.

passages.title: The Lesson of the Lichen
passages.text:
For most of the history of biology, a species was assumed to be a single kind of thing—one organism, one set of instructions, one life lived from birth to death. The lichen quietly refuses this tidy picture. What looks like a single gray-green crust spreading across a stone is in fact a partnership: a fungus and an alga (and often a yeast as well) living so tightly braided together that neither could survive alone in that place. The fungus builds the structure and gathers water; the alga, able to harness sunlight, makes the sugars that feed them both. Neither is in charge. Neither is, strictly speaking, the lichen.

This arrangement was so strange to nineteenth-century naturalists that when the Swiss scientist Simon Schwendener proposed it in 1869, many of his colleagues rejected it outright. A lichen made of two unrelated organisms seemed to them almost a contradiction in terms, like a horse that was also a cart. Surely, they argued, a thing so uniform in appearance must be one thing. But uniformity of appearance, it turns out, is a poor guide to what lies beneath.

Lichens matter far beyond the puzzle they pose. They colonize bare rock and slowly break it into soil, preparing ground for the plants that follow. They can survive drought, extreme cold, and even the vacuum of space, shutting down almost entirely and reviving when conditions improve. And because they absorb whatever falls on them from the air, they serve as sensitive monitors of pollution: where the air turns foul, the lichens vanish first, and their absence is a warning written on the stones.

The lesson of the lichen is not merely botanical. It suggests that some of the most successful life on Earth is not solitary but cooperative—that survival, at least sometimes, is a thing accomplished together.

questions.prompt: The main idea of the passage is that
choices: lichens are the oldest organisms on Earth
a lichen is a cooperative partnership that challenges the idea of a species as a single organism
Simon Schwendener was unfairly ignored by his colleagues
lichens are the best available tool for measuring air pollution
fungi can never survive without algae
questions.explanation: B: the lichen-as-partnership overturns the one-organism assumption. C and D are supporting details; A and E overstate claims the passage never makes.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the alga's main contribution to the lichen is to
choices: gather water
build the physical structure
produce sugars using sunlight
break rock into soil
monitor pollution levels
questions.explanation: C: the alga 'harnesses sunlight' to make sugars for both partners. Gathering water and building structure are the fungus's roles.

questions.prompt: The passage states all of the following about lichens EXCEPT that they
choices: can survive the vacuum of space
help turn bare rock into soil
vanish where the air is polluted
consist of a fungus and an alga
are the direct ancestors of modern plants
questions.explanation: E: the passage never calls lichens plants' ancestors—only that they prepare ground for plants. A through D are stated directly.

questions.prompt: In context, "living so tightly braided together," the word "braided" most nearly means
choices: decorated
interwoven
tied in knots
brightly colored
held separate
questions.explanation: B: 'braided together' means interwoven, reinforced by 'tightly.' E, 'held separate,' is the opposite of the intended meaning.

questions.prompt: The comparison to "a horse that was also a cart" is used to convey that the naturalists
choices: found the two-organism idea to be an absurd contradiction
believed lichens were useful for transportation
thought Schwendener was being dishonest
admired the elegance of the new theory
were certain the theory would soon be proven
questions.explanation: A: the horse/cart image dramatizes a self-contradiction they could not accept. B and D miss the naturalists' disbelief.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward lichens is best described as
choices: alarmed
appreciative and instructive
deeply skeptical
nostalgic
neutral and detached
questions.explanation: B: the closing 'lesson of the lichen' shows admiration and a wish to teach. E is half-right—the tone is engaged, not detached.

passages.title: The Woman Who Drew the Sea
passages.text:
When Marie Tharp began her work in the early 1950s, the floor of the ocean was, to most scientists, a blank. It was assumed to be a featureless plain of mud, dull and more or less flat, sloping gently away from the continents. Tharp, trained in geology at a time when few women were admitted to the field, was not permitted aboard the research ships that gathered the ocean's depth measurements. She was told the sea was no place for a woman. So she stayed ashore and did something that turned out to matter more: she drew.

Sheet by sheet, from thousands of columns of sounding data collected by others, Tharp translated numbers into landscape. Slowly a startling terrain emerged beneath her pencil—not a flat plain at all, but a chain of mountains running down the center of the Atlantic, and, splitting those mountains lengthwise, a deep valley like a wound. That valley, she realized, was a rift, a place where the seafloor was pulling apart.

When she showed her map to her colleague Bruce Heezen, he dismissed the rift as "girl talk." The idea it implied—that continents drifted, that the earth's surface was not fixed but restless—was then considered nearly a heresy. Yet the valley kept appearing wherever the data was plotted. Heezen came around. The map, published in 1957 and later painted in vivid relief, helped persuade a generation of scientists that the theory of plate tectonics was correct.

Tharp's name did not appear on many of the early papers. Recognition came late, and quietly. But the floor of the ocean is no longer a blank, and the person who first drew its true face, working from shore with a pencil and a stubborn eye, deserves to be remembered as one who saw what the sailors sailing over it could not.

questions.prompt: The main idea of the passage is that
choices: Marie Tharp's mapmaking revealed the ocean floor's true shape and helped confirm plate tectonics
women were unfairly barred from research ships in the 1950s
Bruce Heezen discovered the mid-Atlantic rift valley
the ocean floor is a flat, featureless plain of mud
Tharp single-handedly invented the theory of continental drift
questions.explanation: A: Tharp's map exposed real terrain and supported plate tectonics. E overstates (she did not invent the theory); C and D reverse the facts.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, Tharp did not join the research ships because
choices: she had no training in geology
she personally preferred working on shore
she was a woman
she could not interpret sounding data
Heezen forbade her from doing so
questions.explanation: C: 'she was told the sea was no place for a woman.' B is a trap—she stayed ashore, but not out of preference.

questions.prompt: In context, the word "rift" most nearly means
choices: mountain
split or crack
mistake
current
shadow
questions.explanation: B: the rift is a valley where the floor is 'pulling apart'—a split. Mountain (A) and current (D) miss the sense of a break.

questions.prompt: Heezen's remark "girl talk" most strongly suggests that he initially
choices: admired Tharp's careful work
did not take her interpretation seriously
agreed the seafloor was spreading
planned to publish the map immediately
believed Tharp had miscalculated the data
questions.explanation: B: 'girl talk' dismisses her conclusion out of hand. E is a trap—he doubted her credibility, not specifically her arithmetic.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward Tharp is best described as
choices: admiring and appreciative
pitying
neutral and impersonal
critical of her methods
gently amused
questions.explanation: A: the closing tribute ('deserves to be remembered') is plainly admiring. B, 'pitying,' misreads that respect as sympathy.

questions.prompt: The author calls the seafloor "a blank" at the start and "no longer a blank" at the end mainly to
choices: frame Tharp's achievement as filling in what had been unknown
argue that the ocean is empty of life
criticize the whole field of modern oceanography
show that maps are generally unreliable
explain why ships avoided the Atlantic
questions.explanation: A: the shift from 'blank' to 'no longer a blank' frames her as filling in the unknown. B and D distort the metaphor.

passages.title: In Praise of Empty Hours
passages.text:
We have arranged the lives of young people with the thoroughness of a train timetable. From the first bell to the last practice, every hour is accounted for, labeled, and made to justify itself. Homework follows school; a sport or a club follows homework; and what little time remains is often handed over to a screen that fills it obligingly. We call this being productive. I want to argue that it is, in an important sense, a kind of poverty.

The defenders of the packed schedule mean well. They point out, correctly, that opportunities are real and that idle time can curdle into trouble. But they mistake motion for growth. A mind, like a field, needs seasons in which nothing is demanded of it. It is precisely in unclaimed hours—the long, shapeless afternoon, the walk with no destination—that a young person learns to be curious without instruction, to follow a thought simply because it is interesting, to discover what he likes when no one is grading the answer.

Consider how much of what we value most was born in such hours. The inventor tinkering for no reason, the reader wandering into a book she was not assigned, the child inventing a game with rules only she understands—these are not detours from an education. They are its quiet center. Boredom, far from being an enemy, is often the door through which imagination finally walks in.

I do not propose that we abolish effort or ambition. I propose only that we stop treating empty time as wasted time. A schedule with no gaps in it teaches a person to fear the gaps—to reach, whenever a moment opens, for the nearest distraction. Better to leave some hours deliberately blank, and to trust that a young mind, left alone, will not go idle. It will go exploring.

questions.prompt: The main claim of the essay is that
choices: schools should assign significantly less homework
unstructured, unscheduled time is valuable and should be protected for young people
screens are the main cause of students' problems
ambition and effort are harmful to young people
boredom always leads to creativity
questions.explanation: B: the essay defends protecting empty time for the young. A and D are too narrow or extreme; E overstates with 'always.'

questions.prompt: The author concedes that defenders of the packed schedule are correct that
choices: young minds need rest
idle time can lead to trouble
screens fill time obligingly
boredom is the enemy of imagination
empty time is wasted time
questions.explanation: B: he grants that 'idle time can curdle into trouble.' The other options are claims he disputes, not concessions he makes.

questions.prompt: In context, "it is, in an important sense, a kind of poverty," the word "poverty" most nearly means
choices: sadness
laziness
lack or deprivation
danger
wealth
questions.explanation: C: here 'poverty' names a lack or deprivation—of open time. E, 'wealth,' is the direct opposite.

questions.prompt: The comparison of a mind to "a field" that "needs seasons in which nothing is demanded of it" implies that
choices: students should spend more time outdoors
rest and empty time are necessary for the mind to grow
minds and fields are essentially identical
education should follow the farming calendar
young people are naturally lazy
questions.explanation: B: like a fallow field, a mind needs undemanded time to grow. C takes the simile too literally.

questions.prompt: The author's tone toward "the defenders of the packed schedule" is best described as
choices: respectful but disagreeing
openly contemptuous
entirely persuaded
wholly indifferent
fearful
questions.explanation: A: he says they 'mean well' and are 'correct,' yet 'mistake motion for growth'—respectful disagreement. B overstates the contempt.

questions.prompt: The author's primary purpose is to
choices: persuade readers to value unstructured time
describe a typical student's daily schedule
prove that homework has no benefits at all
recommend banning screens for teenagers
explain how schools design their timetables
questions.explanation: A: the piece argues for valuing empty time. B and E are things it mentions in passing, not its purpose; C is too extreme.

passages.title: Late Frost
passages.text:
All night the garden practiced disappearing.
By dawn the lawn had gone to brittle glass,
each blade of grass a small, unbending spear,
the roses stunned mid-bloom, and holding fast.

My grandmother would call this ruin lovely.
She'd stand here at the window, cup in hand,
and name it, quiet: look how the cold has silvered
the very things it came to reprimand.

I used to think she softened what was hard,
inventing kindness where there was only cold.
But now I hold the same chipped cup, and watching
the light walk up the frost as I grow old,

I see she told no comfortable story.
The frost that kills is also what reveals—
the shape of every leaf made suddenly certain,
the garden clearest when it least conceals.

questions.prompt: The central idea of the poem is that
choices: the poem chiefly mourns the death of the speaker's grandmother
the speaker comes to understand the grandmother's view that frost reveals as well as destroys
gardens should be protected from late frost
the speaker deeply regrets having grown old
the grandmother invented false comforts to hide an ugly truth
questions.explanation: B: the speaker finally shares the grandmother's insight that frost reveals as it kills. E reverses the poem's turn; A and D miss its focus.

questions.prompt: In the poem, the grandmother is described as standing
choices: in the garden among the frozen roses
at the window with a cup in hand
beside the speaker as a young child
out in the frost itself
holding a spear of frozen grass
questions.explanation: B: she 'would stand here at the window, cup in hand.' The other placements never appear in the poem.

questions.prompt: In context, "the very things it came to reprimand," the word "reprimand" most nearly means
choices: reward
scold or rebuke
melt away
reveal
protect
questions.explanation: B: to reprimand is to scold; the frost 'silvers' what it came to rebuke. A, 'reward,' is the opposite.

questions.prompt: The lines "I used to think she softened what was hard, / inventing kindness where there was only cold" reveal that the speaker once believed the grandmother
choices: was secretly cruel
saw a beauty that was not really there
hated the garden
understood frost better than anyone
was too old to see clearly
questions.explanation: B: the speaker once thought she 'invented kindness'—saw beauty that wasn't real. A overstates the doubt into cruelty.

questions.prompt: By the end of the poem, the speaker's attitude is best described as
choices: bitter and disappointed
newly appreciative and understanding
fearful of the coming winter
lightly amused
cold and indifferent
questions.explanation: B: the close brings new appreciation and understanding of the grandmother. A, 'bitter,' contradicts the earned insight.

questions.prompt: The shift beginning "But now I hold the same chipped cup" mainly serves to
choices: mark a change from the speaker's old view to a new understanding
introduce a second, colder night
bring a new character into the poem
reject the grandmother's memory entirely
explain the scientific process by which frost forms
questions.explanation: A: 'But now' pivots from the old misjudgment to the new understanding—the poem's turn. B and E miss the shift in thought.

passages.title: The Weight of the Trophy Case
passages.text:
Every afternoon at four, Mr. Alvarez unlocked the trophy case in the school's front hall and polished the tallest cup until the brass threw back his reflection. He had won it thirty-one years ago, running the mile faster than any boy in the county. Now he taught seventh-grade history and coached a track team that had not placed since.

Delia watched him do it once, then twice, then every day for a week. She was new, and quiet, and had learned that the fastest way to understand a place was to notice what its adults refused to stop doing.

"You could just leave it dusty," she said one Thursday, not unkindly.

He didn't turn around. "You polish a thing so you remember it was real."

"Or so everybody else does."

That made him pause, the cloth still against the brass. He set it down and looked at her properly for the first time — a girl who ran the four hundred in practice as though something were chasing her, and who always slowed in the last twenty meters, just before anyone could time her.

"You finish races behind people you could beat," he said. "I've watched."

Delia shrugged. "If I never win, I never have to keep winning."

Mr. Alvarez almost smiled. He picked the cloth back up, but this time he folded it and put it in his pocket. Then he closed the case without polishing anything.

"Tomorrow," he said, "run the whole four hundred. I won't tell you your time unless you ask."

She didn't answer. But the next day, at practice, she did not slow down, and she did not look at the stopwatch in his hand, and he did not look at it either. They both understood, without saying so, that the number was never the point — that the case in the hallway had always held something heavier than a cup.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: a track coach's strategy for winning a county championship
how two people who fear the same thing quietly recognize it in each other
the history of a school and the trophies it has collected over decades
a student's plan to break a longstanding school record
the proper technique for polishing brass and caring for old objects
questions.explanation: The scene centers on Delia and Mr. Alvarez recognizing a shared fear of winning/being measured. (A) and (D) misread; no championship or record is pursued. (C) and (E) name surface details (school history, polishing) that serve the theme rather than being the point.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what does Delia consistently do during practice races?
choices: She refuses to run the four hundred at all
She asks Mr. Alvarez for her time after each race
She slows down in the final twenty meters before she can be timed
She sets a new personal record every afternoon
She polishes the trophy case alongside her coach
questions.explanation: The text states she "always slowed in the last twenty meters, just before anyone could time her." (A) is false — she runs it. (B) reverses the truth; she avoids her time. (D) contradicts her slowing. (E) belongs to Mr. Alvarez, not Delia.

questions.prompt: It can most reasonably be inferred that Mr. Alvarez polishes the trophy every day because
choices: he hopes to sell it and needs it to look valuable
he is required by the school to maintain the front hall
he clings to a past achievement that still defines how he sees himself
he wants to inspire the current team to win more races
he enjoys the quiet routine more than teaching history
questions.explanation: His line "you polish a thing so you remember it was real" and his fading present suggest he clings to a defining past win. (A) and (B) invent motives absent from the text. (D) misreads a private ritual as team motivation. (E) overstates the routine's appeal.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "properly" most nearly means
choices: politely
fully and directly
correctly, by the rules
respectfully
cautiously
questions.explanation: "Looked at her properly for the first time" means he finally saw her fully and directly, having earlier faced the case. (A) and (D) fit "proper" socially but miss the sense of complete attention. (C) suits rules, not gaze. (E) contradicts his newly open notice.

questions.prompt: The attitude Mr. Alvarez shows toward Delia by the end of the passage is best described as
choices: dismissive and impatient
coldly competitive
gently understanding
openly disappointed
anxious and fearful
questions.explanation: He almost smiles, pockets the cloth, and offers to withhold her time — a gesture of quiet empathy. (A) and (D) miss his warming. (B) ignores that he stops competing (with the case, with her). (E) projects Delia's fear onto him rather than his response to it.

questions.prompt: The detail that Mr. Alvarez "closed the case without polishing anything" serves to
choices: show that he has grown too tired to finish his daily chores
signal a shift in him away from clinging to the past
prove that the trophy has finally lost its monetary value
suggest he is angry at Delia for interrupting his ritual
explain why the track team has not placed in years
questions.explanation: Breaking his daily ritual right after Delia's challenge marks his turn away from the past. (A) reads fatigue into a deliberate choice. (C) and (E) draw unrelated conclusions (value, team record). (D) misreads his pause and near-smile as anger.

passages.title: The Weight of the Casserole
passages.text:
Mara had rehearsed the whole drive over: she would set the casserole on the counter, say something light, and leave before her sister could read her face. Nine months of silence did not undo themselves in a doorway.

Dev opened the door holding a dish towel, and for a second neither of them moved. The apartment behind him smelled of onions and something scorched. A child's drawing hung crooked on the refrigerator—her nephew's, she guessed, though she had never met him.

"You didn't have to cook," Dev said.

"I didn't. It's the store kind. I just put it in my own pan so it would look like I tried." The truth slipped out before her strategy could stop it, and she watched it land. Dev almost smiled.

She had come armed with the speech: the apology that wasn't quite an apology, the careful list of who had said what at their father's funeral. But the scorched smell, the crooked drawing, the towel still in his hand—these belonged to a life that had continued without her, ordinary and unremarkable and entirely hers to have missed.

"He's asleep," Dev said, following her glance to the refrigerator. "You can meet him another time. If you want another time."

The casserole was heavy in her arms. She realized she was still holding it, gripping it like a shield or an offering, unsure which. Setting it down would mean deciding.

"I brought enough for four," she said finally, which was not what she had planned to say, and was the only true thing she had managed all week. It meant she was staying. It meant she was asking.

Dev stepped back from the door—not wide, just enough—and let the cooking smell reach her.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: A carefully planned apology repairs a family rupture instantly
A woman's rehearsed strategy for reconciliation dissolves into a more honest, tentative reaching-out
Two siblings argue about events at their father's funeral
A cook is judged for bringing store-bought food to a family gathering
A woman meets her nephew for the first time
questions.explanation: B captures the turn: Mara's script gives way to genuine, uncertain connection. A overstates ('instantly,' 'repairs'); C and E name background details, not the focus; D fixates on the casserole literally rather than what it represents.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why did Mara put the store-bought food into her own pan?
choices: To keep it warm during the drive
So it would look as though she had cooked it herself
Because Dev had asked her to bring a home-cooked meal
To have enough food for four people
Because she had forgotten the original dish
questions.explanation: The text states she used her pan 'so it would look like I tried.' A, C, and E are never stated. D refers to a later, separate line about portions, not her reason for transferring the food.

questions.prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the nine months of silence resulted from
choices: a disagreement connected to their father's funeral
Mara's dislike of her nephew
Dev moving to a new apartment without telling her
a quarrel over who would cook holiday meals
Mara's long-distance job
questions.explanation: The 'careful list of who had said what at their father's funeral' points to a funeral-related rift. B is impossible—she's never met the child. C, D, and E invent causes with no textual support.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "armed" most nearly means
choices: physically weaponed
prepared and defensive
injured
affectionate
exhausted
questions.explanation: Mara came 'armed with the speech'—equipped with a defensive script, matching 'prepared and defensive.' A is the literal military sense, wrong here. C, D, and E ignore the context of a rehearsed, guarded approach.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward Mara can best be described as
choices: mocking and dismissive
sympathetic and closely observant
coldly detached
openly celebratory
impatient and scolding
questions.explanation: The narration tracks Mara's inner conflict with tender precision ('shield or an offering, unsure which'), signaling sympathy and close attention. A and E misread the gentle tone; C ignores the intimacy; D overstates—the ending is hopeful but restrained, not celebratory.

questions.prompt: The detail of Mara 'gripping it like a shield or an offering' primarily serves to
choices: show that the casserole is physically too heavy for her
emphasize her uncertainty about whether she comes to defend herself or to make peace
reveal that she regrets bringing food at all
suggest she is planning to leave immediately
prove she is a skilled and generous cook
questions.explanation: 'Shield or an offering, unsure which' externalizes her indecision between self-defense and reconciliation. A reads the image literally; C and D contradict her choice to stay; E misuses the gesture as praise of her cooking rather than emotion.

passages.title: The Reasoning of the Brainless
passages.text:
The slime mold is neither plant nor animal, and it owns not a single neuron. For most of its life it is a smear of yellow goo, oozing across rotting logs in search of bacteria. Nothing about it suggests intelligence. And yet, in laboratories, this humble organism has begun to embarrass us.

Place a slime mold in a maze with food at two exits, and it will, over hours, retract from the dead-end corridors and stretch its body along the single shortest path between the meals. Offer it a scattering of oat flakes arranged like the cities around Tokyo, and it will connect them with a network of tubes that rivals the actual rail system engineers spent decades designing. It has no map, no memory as we understand memory, no plan. It simply grows toward what nourishes it and withers from what does not.

We are tempted to call this problem-solving, and perhaps we should resist. The slime mold is not clever in the way a raven is clever; it does not weigh options. But our discomfort reveals something about us, not about the mold. We have quietly assumed that good decisions require a brain — that reasoning must look like thinking. The mold makes no such assumption. It distributes its "choices" across its whole rippling body, and arrives at answers we would call wise had a mind produced them.

This should unsettle the tidy hierarchy we have built, with human cleverness perched at the top and everything else arranged obediently below. Nature, it seems, has discovered many roads to a good solution, and only one of them runs through anything resembling a head. The brainless smear on the log is not aspiring to be like us. If anything, it suggests that intelligence was never ours to define.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: proving that slime molds are smarter than ravens
using the slime mold to challenge the assumption that intelligence requires a brain
describing the decades engineers spent designing Tokyo's rail system
urging scientists to conduct more experiments on simple organisms
explaining how plants and animals evolved from a common ancestor
questions.explanation: The passage builds toward the claim that good solutions need not run through a brain. (A) overreaches; the author explicitly says the mold is not clever like a raven. (C) and (D) are supporting details, not the point.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, a slime mold placed in a maze with food at two exits will
choices: memorize the single correct route in advance
withdraw from dead ends and extend along the shortest path to the food
find the exit instantly upon entering
fail to move at all without a map to follow
eat through the walls of the maze to reach the food
questions.explanation: The text says it will "retract from the dead-end corridors and stretch its body along the single shortest path." (A) is contradicted ("no map, no memory"); (C) ignores "over hours"; (D) misreads its lack of a map as paralysis.

questions.prompt: The author suggests that our "discomfort" with calling the mold's behavior problem-solving
choices: proves the slime mold is secretly conscious
reveals more about human assumptions than about the mold itself
is a feeling the slime mold experiences too
will disappear as laboratory equipment improves
stems from badly designed experiments
questions.explanation: The author states the discomfort "reveals something about us, not about the mold." (A) overstates; the author withholds the word "clever." (C) is absurd for a brainless organism, and (D) and (E) invent causes the passage never raises.

questions.prompt: As used in the final paragraph, the word "tidy" most nearly means
choices: spotlessly clean
physically small
neatly ordered
strictly honest
easily broken
questions.explanation: "The tidy hierarchy... with human cleverness perched at the top and everything else arranged obediently below" describes a neat ordering. (A) is "tidy's" everyday sense of clean but fits a room, not a hierarchy; (B) and (D) miss the meaning entirely.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward the slime mold is best described as
choices: dismissive and mocking
alarmed and fearful
appreciative and provoked to reflection
detached and purely clinical
nostalgic and sentimental
questions.explanation: The author admires the mold's "wise" solutions and lets it "unsettle" human assumptions. (A) misreads "humble" and "smear" as scorn; (B) mistakes intellectual unsettling for fear; (D) ignores the clear stance the author takes.

questions.prompt: The passage indicates that the slime mold lacks all of the following EXCEPT
choices: neurons
a map
memory as we understand it
a body
a plan
questions.explanation: The passage says the mold distributes its choices "across its whole rippling body," so it clearly has a body. Every other option is explicitly named as something the mold does not possess ("not a single neuron"; "no map, no memory..., no plan").

passages.title: The Reluctant Clock
passages.text:
For most of human history, a fossil was an embarrassment. Farmers turning up a stone shell on a hilltop, hundreds of miles from any sea, did not conclude that the hill had once lain underwater. That explanation was unavailable to them—not because they lacked intelligence, but because they lacked time. Their world was young, a few thousand years old, and a few thousand years is not enough to drown a mountain and raise it again.

So the shells were explained away. They were "sports of nature," playful accidents in which stone imitated life for no reason at all. They were seeds of rock that grew underground. They were the litter of Noah's flood. Each explanation preserved the shortness of the past by treating the fossil as a curiosity rather than a witness.

The turn came slowly, and it came from quarrymen and surveyors more than from philosophers. Men who cut roads and dug canals saw the same layers stacked in the same order, mile after mile, each thin as a page. You cannot lay down a thousand identical pages in an afternoon. The rock, it seemed, was keeping a record, and the record was long—unimaginably long.

To read it, people had to grant the Earth something they had denied it: patience. The fossil had not changed. What changed was the frame around it. Once the past was allowed to be deep, the shell on the hilltop stopped being a joke and became a clock—slow, honest, and indifferent to how badly its testimony frightened us.

This is the quiet lesson of the fossil: a fact can sit in your hand for centuries, fully visible, and still remain unseen. We do not observe the world so much as we permit ourselves to. The evidence waits. It is remarkably good at waiting.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: Fossils ultimately proved that the biblical flood had occurred.
A fact may be plainly visible for centuries yet go unrecognized until a new framework permits people to understand it.
Quarrymen and surveyors were more intelligent than the philosophers of their age.
Scientists have determined that the Earth is billions of years old.
Fossils are best understood as the scattered litter left behind by Noah's flood.
questions.explanation: B captures the essay's thesis, stated in the final paragraph. A and E are discarded early explanations, not the point. C overstates a detail about who drove the change. D is never claimed; the passage speaks only of 'unimaginably long,' not a figure.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the shift toward understanding fossils came chiefly from which group?
choices: Philosophers who reasoned about nature
Farmers who plowed the hillsides
Quarrymen and surveyors who cut roads and canals
Priests who interpreted scripture
Kings who funded expeditions
questions.explanation: The passage says the turn 'came from quarrymen and surveyors more than from philosophers,' making C correct and A its explicit foil. Farmers only found the shells without drawing conclusions; priests and kings are never mentioned in this role.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that early people rejected the idea that a hilltop had once been underwater mainly because
choices: they had never traveled far enough to see the ocean
their conception of the past was too short to allow so slow a change
they did not trust the reports of quarrymen and surveyors
fossils were too rare for anyone to study them carefully
their religion strictly forbade the study of stones
questions.explanation: The text ties the rejection to lacking 'time'—a young world can't 'drown a mountain and raise it again.' B follows directly. A and D invent obstacles never mentioned. E overreads: religion supplied one explanation but is not said to forbid inquiry.

questions.prompt: As used in the phrase "sports of nature," the word "sports" most nearly means
choices: organized athletic contests
playful freaks or accidents
solemn warnings
careful written records
valuable buried treasures
questions.explanation: The phrase is glossed in the same sentence as 'playful accidents' in which stone imitates life 'for no reason at all,' so B fits. A is the common modern meaning—the trap. C, D, and E contradict the dismissive, trivializing sense the author gives the term.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward the fossil's "testimony" is best described as
choices: dismissive and impatient
anxious and fearful
respectful and reflective
detached and uninterested
mocking and playful
questions.explanation: The author calls the record 'honest' and draws a 'quiet lesson' from it, a respectful, thoughtful stance—C. B misreads: it is people, not the author, who are 'frightened.' A, D, and E describe the old dismissive view the essay works to overturn.

questions.prompt: The passage attributes all of the following explanations to early people EXCEPT:
choices: Fossils were sports of nature.
Fossils were seeds of rock that grew underground.
Fossils were litter left by Noah's flood.
Fossils were clocks that recorded the Earth's deep past.
Stone had imitated life for no reason at all.
questions.explanation: A, B, C, and E are the early 'explained away' notions listed in paragraph two. D is the later understanding that arrives only after the turn, when the past was 'allowed to be deep'—so it is the exception the question seeks.

passages.title: The Berry Rows
passages.text:
Every August my grandmother woke me before the heat could settle, and we went to the blackberry rows behind her house while the grass was still cold enough to sting. She moved down the canes with a patience I mistook, then, for slowness. I wanted to fill my pail and be finished; she wanted the morning itself. "The ripe ones let go easy," she told me. "If you have to pull, it isn't ready." I pulled anyway, and the hard berries stayed sour in my mouth all day, a small private punishment I never confessed.

She wore my grandfather's old canvas gloves, though the thorns still found her wrists. When I asked why she didn't buy new ones, she only laughed and said these knew the work. I thought this was a foolish thing to say about gloves. I was nine, and I believed that objects were only objects, that a tool was a tool and nothing more.

We filled our pails and carried them to the porch, where she rinsed the berries and set aside the best ones—not for eating, I learned, but for the neighbors down the road who had no garden of their own. This annoyed me. I had done the reaching; I had earned the stains on my fingers. Why should the sweetest berries go to strangers?

I understand now what I could not then. She was teaching me something without the indignity of a lesson, letting the morning do the speaking. The gloves that knew the work. The ripe ones that let go easy. The best fruit given away. It was all one quiet argument about how a person ought to live, and she trusted me to overhear it eventually.

She has been gone four years. Last August I woke before the heat and went looking for blackberries, and I did not pull a single one.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: the difficulty of picking blackberries in the summer heat
how a grandmother taught the narrator values indirectly, understood only in hindsight
an argument between a grandmother and grandson that was never resolved
the importance of using the proper equipment for gardening
a family's tradition of donating fruit to their neighbors
questions.explanation: The whole memoir builds to the narrator finally grasping a lesson taught without lecturing (B). A, D, and E are literal details lifted from single moments, not the point. C misreads: the tension dissolves into understanding, and no ongoing quarrel exists.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the grandmother set aside the best berries in order to
choices: eat them herself at breakfast
make preserves for the winter
give them to neighbors who had no garden
reward the narrator for his work
save them for her late husband
questions.explanation: The text states the best ones went to neighbors with no garden (C). D tempts because the narrator felt he had earned them, but the passage denies this. E and B invent details never mentioned; the grandfather appears only through the gloves.

questions.prompt: The final sentence, in which the narrator "did not pull a single one," most strongly suggests that he
choices: could no longer locate any ripe berries
has come to understand and honor his grandmother's lesson
no longer enjoys the taste of blackberries
was afraid of the thorns now that he had no gloves
will pick berries only when someone teaches him again
questions.explanation: Refusing to pull enacts her rule—ripe ones let go easy—showing he finally lives the lesson (B). A reads the line literally as a failed search. C and D substitute fear or distaste for the reverence the mournful tone actually conveys.

questions.prompt: As used in the first paragraph, "mistook" most nearly means
choices: resented
misinterpreted
admired
forgave
noticed
questions.explanation: He wrongly read her patience as slowness, so "mistook" means misinterpreted (B). E tempts because he did observe her, but the word signals a judgment that was wrong, not mere noticing. A and C impose feelings the sentence does not support.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward his nine-year-old self is best described as
choices: bitterly ashamed
gently regretful and understanding
proud and wholly unapologetic
detached and indifferent
amused and dismissive
questions.explanation: The adult forgives his childish impatience while quietly mourning it (B). A overstates the mild regret into bitterness. C reverses his stance, and E's dismissiveness contradicts the tender, reflective close about a grandmother now gone.

questions.prompt: The grandmother's remark that the old gloves "knew the work" primarily serves to
choices: explain her reluctance to spend money on new gloves
illustrate her belief that objects carry meaning beyond their use, a value the narrator later grasps
account for the scratches the thorns left on her wrists
prove how deeply she still missed her late husband
reveal that she disliked going shopping
questions.explanation: The line embodies the theme that things hold meaning—which the narrator dismisses at nine and honors later (B). A restates his childhood misreading of her as merely frugal. C is literal, and D and E infer motives the passage never establishes.

passages.title: Salvage
passages.text:
My father kept a shallow bedroom drawer of small things that had failed:
the silver wristwatch whose thin hands forgot, one winter, to keep their word,
the tabletop radio that hummed all evening but somehow caught no living station,
a rusted hinge, a blackened fuse, the carefully folded wing of one cracked bird
he had whittled slowly by himself and dropped. He liked to call the drawer his archive,
a private, patient, almost tender census of the household's broken and its bent,
and on the long slow Sundays after church he would lay them gently out like patients
and coax them, screw by stubborn screw, back toward the shape of whatever each had meant.
He rarely won a single one of them. The silver wristwatch stayed forever fixed at seven.
The radio, for all his care, held onto nothing but its low, deliberate, level moan.
But I have come, these later colder years, to understand he never once was truly mending—
he simply could not bring himself to stand and leave a small and hurting thing alone.
So now, when kinder, gentler voices at my shoulder lean in close and tell me to release it,
tell me the hour is over now, and that some things were never really mine to do,
I set my jaw in that same stubborn, quiet, disbelieving way I watched him use to,
and bend myself down low above the cooling wreckage of another ordinary evening,
and turn it slowly in the yellow lamplight, over once, and under, and at last back through,
and ask it, almost tenderly, the way he might have: tell me, what is it you are reaching to?

questions.prompt: The poem is primarily concerned with
choices: a father's professional career as a machine repairman
the way the speaker learned to fix household appliances
how the speaker inherited the father's refusal to abandon what is broken
the speaker's grief over a father lost to a long illness
the contrast between old technology and new
questions.explanation: The turn shows the speaker now tending 'wreckage' as the father did, so (C) captures the inherited stance. (A) and (B) take the repairing literally though he 'rarely won'; (D) invents a death; (E) mistakes surface objects for the point.
