questions.prompt: According to the poem, the wristwatch
choices: was eventually repaired by the father
originally belonged to the speaker
stayed permanently stopped at seven
hummed but caught no living station
was carved by the father himself
questions.explanation: The text states the watch 'stayed forever fixed at seven,' making (C) correct. (A) contradicts 'He rarely won'; (D) describes the radio; (E) describes the carved bird; (B) is never stated.

questions.prompt: The father's habit of keeping objects he could not repair most strongly suggests that he
choices: hoped to sell the broken pieces for parts
valued the act of tending more than actual success
could not afford to replace the items
deeply distrusted all modern machines
meant to teach the speaker a useful trade
questions.explanation: Since he 'rarely won' yet kept coaxing, the caring matters more than fixing, supporting (B). (C) invents poverty; (E) reads it as a lesson the poem never claims; (A) contradicts his 'tender' keeping; (D) overreads one broken radio.

questions.prompt: In line 6, the word "census" most nearly means
choices: an official count of a population
a government-issued survey form
a careful inventory or catalog
a solemn ceremony of mourning
a harsh and final judgment
questions.explanation: Here 'census' names his gathered collection of broken things, so (C) fits. (A) and (B) are the ordinary dictionary sense that ignores context; (D) and (E) import a grim tone the affectionate 'tender' rules out.

questions.prompt: The speaker's attitude toward the father is best described as
choices: resentful and dismissive
detached and clinical
mocking but secretly affectionate
tender and admiring
fearful and resentful
questions.explanation: Words like 'almost tender' and the speaker imitating the father's 'stubborn, quiet' way signal warmth and respect, so (D). (A) and (E) misread affection as resentment; (B) ignores clear feeling; (C) is tempting but nothing in the poem mocks him.

questions.prompt: The lines beginning "But I have come...to understand he never once was truly mending" chiefly serve to
choices: reveal that the father eventually succeeded
shift the poem from describing a habit to interpreting its meaning
introduce a wholly new character
openly criticize the father's stubbornness
explain how the objects were finally discarded
questions.explanation: This is the poem's turn, moving from what the father did to what it meant, so (B). (A) reverses the text; (D) misreads admiration as criticism; (C) adds no character; (E) is never mentioned, since the objects are kept, not discarded.

passages.title: The Atom
passages.text:
All matter is made of atoms. Different kinds of atoms can combine to form new substances. The page you are reading is made up of billions of atoms. So are you. An atom is an exceedingly tiny thing. For example, it would take a line of about 200 million hydrogen atoms, the smallest kind of atom, to span the distance of one inch.

In the nineteenth century, many scientists thought that atoms were indivisible blobs of matter. Now we know that atoms are far more complicated. We also know that under the right conditions certain atoms can be split into smaller particles. When this happens, energy is given off. In certain circumstances it is possible to capture this energy in the form of heat, then use the heat to make electricity.

questions.prompt: This passage is primarily about
choices: A) how matter is made
B) what atoms are like
C) nineteenth-century scientific discoveries
D) how to generate electricity
E) atomic energy heating systems
questions.explanation: The passage moves from what atoms are to splitting them for energy, but its through-line is the nature of atoms. (D) covers only the last sentence.

questions.prompt: As used in line 3, "exceedingly" means
choices: A) somewhat
B) fortunately
C) extremely
D) unexpectedly
E) increasingly
questions.explanation: 'An atom is an exceedingly tiny thing' — exceedingly = extremely. (E) 'increasingly' implies change over time, which the sentence doesn't.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from this passage that all of the following statements about atoms are correct EXCEPT:
choices: A) Solids, liquids, and gases are all made of atoms.
B) Atoms are composed of smaller particles.
C) Matter can be converted into energy.
D) Living things are composed of atoms.
E) All atoms are of the same size.
questions.explanation: 'hydrogen atoms, the smallest kind of atom' implies atoms differ in size, so (E) is false — the EXCEPT answer. The other four are all stated or implied.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what happens when an atom is broken apart?
choices: A) It forms small hydrogen atoms.
B) It captures heat.
C) It turns into electricity.
D) It gives off energy.
E) It creates billions of new atoms.
questions.explanation: 'certain atoms can be split... When this happens, energy is given off.' (B)/(C) are later steps done with that energy, not what happens at the split itself.

questions.prompt: The author's style is best described as
choices: A) dramatic
B) sarcastic
C) mysterious
D) informative
E) poetic
questions.explanation: Plain factual explanation of atoms — informative. (E) 'poetic' and (A) 'dramatic' mistake clarity for flourish.

passages.title: The Village of Florida
passages.text:
I was born on November 30, 1835, in the village of Florida, Missouri. My parents had moved to Missouri in the thirties; I do not remember just when, for I was not born then and cared nothing for such things. The village contained a hundred people and I increased the population by one percent. It is more than many of the best people in history could have done for a town. There is no record of a person doing as much—not even Shakespeare.

Recently someone in Missouri sent me a picture of the house I was born in. Heretofore I had always stated that it was a palace but I shall be more guarded now.

The village had two streets, each a couple of hundred yards long; the rest of the avenues were lanes, with rail fences and cornfields on either side. Both the streets and the lanes were paved with the same material—tough black mud in wet times, deep dust in dry.

Most of the houses were of logs—all of them, indeed, except three or four; these latter were frame ones. There were none of brick and none of stone. There was a log church, with a puncheon floor and slab benches. A puncheon floor is made of logs whose upper surfaces have been chipped flat with an adz. The cracks between the logs were not filled; there was no carpet; consequently, if you dropped anything smaller than a peach it was likely to go through. The church was perched upon short sections of logs, which elevated it two or three feet from the ground. Hogs slept under there, and whenever the dogs got after them during services, the minister had to wait till the disturbance was over. In winter there was always a refreshing breeze up through the puncheon floor; in summer there were fleas enough for all.

questions.prompt: The house that the author was born in was probably constructed of
choices: A) logs
B) concrete
C) stone
D) bricks
E) mud
questions.explanation: 'Most of the houses were of logs—all... except three or four.' Odds are the author's was one of the many log houses, not the few frame ones. No brick or stone existed.

questions.prompt: The hogs referred to in the passage were
choices: A) near the house
B) under the church
C) in a cornfield
D) by the barnyard
E) on the street
questions.explanation: 'Hogs slept under there' — 'there' being under the log church. (A) the house is never where the hogs are placed.

questions.prompt: According to the author, the hogs created a disturbance because
choices: A) fleas were annoying them
B) people were feeding them
C) the choir was singing
D) dogs were chasing them
E) they were cold
questions.explanation: 'whenever the dogs got after them during services, the minister had to wait.' Dogs chasing the hogs caused the disturbance, not the fleas (A).

questions.prompt: An "adz" is probably a type of
choices: A) floor covering
B) cutting tool
C) church bench
D) hog
E) peach
questions.explanation: 'chipped flat with an adz' — a tool used to shape wood, so a cutting tool. Context (making a floor) rules out the non-tool options.

questions.prompt: According to the author, the most valuable contribution he made to the village of Florida was
choices: A) building a palace
B) carpeting the church
C) paving the streets
D) introducing Shakespeare to the town
E) increasing the population by one percent
questions.explanation: 'I increased the population by one percent. It is more than many of the best people in history could have done.' The joke names this his great contribution.

questions.prompt: The mood of this passage can best be described as
choices: A) humorous
B) argumentative
C) analytical
D) tense
E) somber
questions.explanation: Hogs under the church, fleas 'enough for all,' the mock-boast about Shakespeare — the mood is humorous. Nothing argumentative or somber.

passages.title: The Deer Underpasses
passages.text: From 1970, when a 55-mile stretch of Interstate 80 in Wyoming opened, to 1977, more than 1,000 deer were killed as they crossed between their winter and summer homes. Scientists counted deer tracks in the snow and found that 80 percent of the deer crossed the highway in a single eight mile stretch. Passageways under the highway were built specifically for the animals to use, and an eight-mile-long fence along both sides of the road was erected to funnel the animals into the underpasses. At first the deer appeared apprehensive and paced up and down the fence for as long as three months before crossing. Finally bait was used to lure stragglers into the underpasses. In subsequent years bait proved to be unnecessary, and the number of deer killed along the fenced stretch has been reduced from an average of 100 per year to near zero.

questions.prompt: How did the scientists discover where most of the deer were crossing the highway?
choices: A) They observed the deer crossing the highway.
B) They counted the deer that stopped to eat the bait.
C) They counted deer tracks in the snow.
D) They knew there was only one stretch of road where the deer could cross.
E) They counted the bodies of deer killed by cars.
questions.explanation: 'Scientists counted deer tracks in the snow and found that 80 percent crossed in a single eight-mile stretch.' (E) counting bodies isn't how they found the crossing spot.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that the deer did not use the underpasses at first because they
choices: A) were frightened by the cars going overhead
B) had seen other deer killed near there
C) had not encountered underpasses before
D) did not like the fences leading to the underpasses
E) were frightened away by the smell of the bait
questions.explanation: The underpasses were newly built and unfamiliar, so the deer 'appeared apprehensive' and had to be lured with bait. (A) 'frightened by cars overhead' is never supported by the text.

questions.prompt: What made the deer finally use the underpasses?
choices: A) After three months they got tired of pacing along the fences.
B) Scientists herded them together and pushed them through.
C) One deer led the others through.
D) Food they liked was placed nearby.
E) They found better feeding grounds on the opposite side of the highway.
questions.explanation: 'Finally bait was used to lure stragglers into the underpasses.' Food they wanted drew them in; the pacing (A) is what happened before, not what worked.

questions.prompt: As it is used in line 6, the word "funnel" most nearly means
choices: A) summon
B) receive
C) guide
D) pour
E) transplant
questions.explanation: A fence 'to funnel the animals into the underpasses' = to guide them. (D) 'pour' fits a literal funnel but not animals being steered.

questions.prompt: Which of the following questions is answered by the information in the passage?
choices: A) How long did it take to build the underpasses?
B) How far do the deer travel each year to reach their winter homes?
C) Where was the deer's winter home located?
D) Who assessed the value of the underpasses?
E) What was the reduction in the annual rate of deer killed in car accidents in this area?
questions.explanation: The passage gives the drop 'from an average of 100 per year to near zero,' answering (E). It never states build time, migration distance, or who assessed the project.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: A) Many deer are killed annually in highway accidents.
B) People have found a way to help deer cross roads safely.
C) Scientists have discovered that deer migrate.
D) Many people in Wyoming care about wildlife.
E) It was a mistake to build Interstate 80.
questions.explanation: The whole passage describes engineering a safe crossing for deer — (B). (A) is just the problem that setup solves; (E) is never claimed.

passages.title: Anthony at Her Trial
passages.text:
The following speech was delivered by Susan B. Anthony at her trial in 1873.

Friends and fellow-citizens: I stand before you tonight under indictment for the alleged crime of having voted at the last Presidential election [1872], without having a lawful right to vote. It shall be my work this evening to prove to you that in thus voting, I not only committed no crime, but, instead, simply exercised my citizen's rights, guaranteed to me and all United States citizens by the National Constitution.

It was we, the people; not we, the White male citizens; nor yet we, the male citizens; but we, the whole people, who formed the Union. And we formed it, not to give the blessings of liberty, but to secure them; not to the half of ourselves but to the whole people—women as well as men. And it is a downright mockery to talk to women of their enjoyment of the blessings of liberty, while they are denied the use of the only means of securing them ...—the ballot....

The only question left to be settled now is: Are women persons? And I hardly believe any of our opponents will have the hardihood to say they are not. Being persons, then, women are citizens; and no State has a right to make any law, or to enforce any old law, that shall abridge their privileges or immunities.

questions.prompt: In line 1, Anthony's use of "fellow-citizens" is ironic for which of the following reasons?
choices: A) She was not fully a citizen because she had no right to vote.
B) The audience included people not of her sex, race, or religion.
C) Most of the citizens in the audience were females.
D) Those in the audience who were her friends were not necessarily citizens.
E) At that time women were not allowed to make speeches on public issues.
questions.explanation: Calling them 'fellow-citizens' is ironic because, denied the vote, she was not treated as a full citizen herself — (A), the point her whole speech drives at.

questions.prompt: What does Anthony mean by "I stand before you tonight under indictment" (line 1)?
choices: A) She had been framed by her opponents.
B) She was already in jail.
C) She had been falsely accused of voting in the last election.
D) She was on trial and might be sent to prison.
E) She had been allowed to speak by special permission.
questions.explanation: 'Under indictment' means formally charged and on trial, facing punishment — (D). (C) is wrong: she never denies voting; she disputes that voting was a crime.

questions.prompt: How does Anthony's speech reflect the idea in a speech by Abraham Lincoln in which he defended "government of the people, by the people, for the people"?
choices: A) It points out that all citizens should be able to elect their government.
B) It shows that educated women can solve the problems of government:
C) It shows that women can have only a theoretical interest in affairs of state.
D) It shows that by voting Anthony was over-reaching her rights as a citizen.
E) It demonstrates that laws in violation of the Constitution are null and void.
questions.explanation: Lincoln's 'government... by the people' matches her claim that 'the whole people—women as well as men' should share in it, i.e. all citizens should help elect it — (A).

questions.prompt: Why does Anthony say that women's right to vote is guaranteed by the Constitution?
choices: A) The Constitution explicitly gave women the right to vote.
B) One of the blessings of liberty is being allowed to vote.
C) The Constitution gave states the power to authorize women to vote.
D) Only children and criminals were denied the right to vote.
E) The Constitution begins, "We, the people," which includes women.
questions.explanation: 'It was we, the people... the whole people, who formed the Union'; being persons, women are among 'we the people' — (E). (A) is false: the Constitution didn't explicitly enfranchise women.

questions.prompt: The purpose of Anthony's speech was to
choices: A) influence the women on the jury in her favor
B) prove that the right to vote would produce racial equality
C) demonstrate that she and other women had the right to vote
D) convince people of the need for economic justice: equal pay for equal work
E) convince the jury that voting was only a tool to be used by women to gain other rights
questions.explanation: 'It shall be my work this evening to prove... I simply exercised my citizen's rights.' Her aim is to show women already had the right to vote — (C).

passages.title: The Carrier of Embers
passages.text:
The Mahto band kept to a certain order when they traveled on the plain. And so Wanagi, the young seer, walked in back of the leader. They assigned this place in the procession of seventy persons to the one who carries the smoldering wood, the source of a cooking fire for these twelve Mahto families upon their arrival at the summer campground.

The grandfathers had said that only good hands—hands that never kill—shall hold these smoldering remains of the winter campfire, a symbol of the continuity of the people. And that the Mahto women, when they start their new campfires from these embers, shall offer thanks to Pte—the one and the herd—for Pte, the true meat, shall sustain each generation of Dakota.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, all of the following are part of the rituals of the Mahto EXCEPT the
choices: A) order in which they walk
B) role of the person who carries the embers
C) source of fire for the new campfires
D) size of their families
E) prayers that are offered when the campfire is started
questions.explanation: Family size is mentioned ('twelve Mahto families') but not as a ritual; walking order, the ember-carrier's role, the new-fire source, and the thanks-prayer all are. So (D).

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the smoldering wood must be
choices: A) kept safe from other tribes
B) carried only by a male
C) prayed over by the tribe as a group
D) used only in ceremonies
E) carried by someone who has never killed
questions.explanation: 'only good hands—hands that never kill—shall hold these smoldering remains.' The ember-carrier must never have killed — (E).

questions.prompt: The embers Wanagi carries symbolize which of the following to the Dakota? I. The beginning of hunting season II. The thankfulness of Pte III. The wisdom of their leader IV. The continuity of their people
choices: A) I only
B) II only
C) IV only
D) III and IV only
E) I, II, and IV only
questions.explanation: The embers are 'a symbol of the continuity of the people' — IV only, (C). The thanks to Pte is offered by the women, not symbolized by the embers themselves.

questions.prompt: This passage is primarily about
choices: A) a Native American seer named Wanagi
B) the significance of fire to the Dakota
C) status symbols among Native Americans
D) the military history of the Dakota
E) the westward migration of the Dakota
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the smoldering embers and what fire means to the Dakota — (B). Wanagi (A) only appears as the one who carries them.

questions.prompt: The attitude of the writer toward the subject is
choices: A) respectful
B) calculating
C) casual
D) accusing
E) cautious
questions.explanation: The reverent account of 'good hands' and thanks to Pte shows a respectful attitude — (A). Nothing is accusing or casual.

passages.title: A Wind Sways the Pines
passages.text:
A wind sways the pines,
    And below,
Not a breath of wild air
Still as the mosses that glow

On the flooring and over the lines
Of the roots here and there.
The pine-tree drops its dead;
They are quiet as under the sea.
Overhead, overhead
Rushes life in a race,
As the clouds the clouds chase;
    And we go,
And we drop like the fruits of the tree,
    Even we,
    Even so.

—George Meridith
The Victorian Age, by Bowyer and Brooks.

questions.prompt:
In this poem, our busy lives are compared to which of the following?
I. The mosses that glow
II. The clouds blowing past
III. Activity under the sea
choices: A) I only
B) II only
C) III only
D) I and II only
E) I, II, and III
questions.explanation: 'Rushes life in a race, / As the clouds the clouds chase' — busy life is likened to the passing clouds (II). The mosses and the sea image stand for stillness, not busyness.

questions.prompt: "The pine-tree drops its dead" (line 7) suggests that
choices: A) wind has damaged the tree beyond repair
B) the tree will no longer produce cones
C) the tree is dying and falling over
D) pine trees do not change color
E) pine needles and cones fall from the tree
questions.explanation: 'The pine-tree drops its dead' — the needles and cones it sheds, which lie 'quiet as under the sea' — (E). The tree itself is not dying (C).

questions.prompt: In this poem, the wind above and the stillness below most probably represent
choices: A) the sky and the sea
B) restlessness and contentment
C) happiness and sadness
D) human life and death
E) youth and old age
questions.explanation: The swaying wind above (motion, life) set against the stillness below (like 'under the sea') stands for human life and death — (D), confirmed by 'we drop like the fruits of the tree.'

questions.prompt: With which of the following statements about death would the speaker be most likely to agree?
choices: A) People should try not to think about death.
B) Having no appreciation of nature is like being dead.
C) Death is an inevitable part of life.
D) Death often comes suddenly and unannounced.
E) Death usually comes when life is quiet and still.
questions.explanation: 'And we go, / And we drop like the fruits of the tree, / Even we, / Even so' presents death as a natural, inevitable part of life — (C).

passages.title: Chopin at the Piano
passages.text: Chopin's own playing was the counterpart of his personality. All that could be distinguished in the man was apparent in the pianist—the same precision; the horror of excess and all that is careless and uncontrolled; the same good manners and high tone of character, combined with poetic warmth and a romantic fervor of expression. No one had ever heard such polished playing, although others could make a more overwhelming impression by their rush and violence. It is a mistake, encouraged by sentimental legend, to believe that Chopin's playing was limited by a delicacy which was equivalent to weakness. Even in the last stages of tuberculosis, he could rally and play with an energy that surprised the audience, who saw in front of them "a slight, frail-looking person." At his final public appearance in November 1848, less than a year before the end, he managed to play "with his usual brilliance."

questions.prompt: Although he performed while seriously ill with tuberculosis, Chopin surprised the audience with his
choices: A) frail appearance
B) polish
C) loss of control
D) violence
E) energetic playing
questions.explanation: 'he could rally and play with an energy that surprised the audience.' His energetic playing (E) is the surprise, set against his frail look.

questions.prompt: The "end" mentioned in line 11 most likely refers to
choices: A) Chopin's retirement from public life
B) the decline of Chopin's genius
C) the invalidism caused by his illness
D) Chopin's death in 1849
E) the end of the concert tour
questions.explanation: 'his final public appearance in November 1848, less than a year before the end' — the end is his death (in 1849), (D), not a mere retirement or tour's end.

questions.prompt: This passage deals primarily with Chopin's
choices: A) musical compositions
B) musical performance
C) debilitating illness
D) aristocratic personality
E) romantic fervor
questions.explanation: The passage is about how Chopin played — his performance and its qualities — (B), not his compositions or personality in the abstract.

questions.prompt: When discussing Chopin, the author's tone in this passage could best be described as
choices: A) admiring
B) brusque
C) ironic
D) hesitant
E) anguished
questions.explanation: Praise of his 'polished playing' and correction of the 'sentimental legend' that he was weak show an admiring tone — (A).

questions.prompt: The author would most likely agree that
choices: A) Chopin was made seriously ill by widespread misinterpretation of his music
B) Chopin's behavior alternated between extreme self-control and outbursts of violence
C) Chopin's character was a commendable blend of sensitivity and restraint
D) the delicacy of Chopin's playing was due in large part to his health problems
E) Chopin prolonged his life in spite of his illness by keeping active
questions.explanation: His playing showed 'precision; the horror of excess' together with 'poetic warmth' — a blend of restraint and sensitivity, (C). The passage rejects (D) and never claims (B) or (E).

passages.title: Wit and Humor
passages.text: I am not sure that I can draw an exact line between wit and humor, but I am positive that humor is the more comfortable and livable quality. Humorous persons, if their gift is genuine, are always agreeable companions and they sit through the evening best. They have pleasant mouths turned up at the corners. To those corners the great Master of marionettes has fixed the strings and holds them with nimble fingers that twitch them at the slightest jest. But the mouth of a merely witty person is hard and sour until the moment of its discharge. Nor is the flash from a witty person always comforting, whereas a humorous person radiates a general pleasure and is like another candle in the room.

questions.prompt: According to the author, a humorous person is like
choices: A) an expensive gift
B) a loaded gun
C) an unusual puppet
D) a comforting light
E) a mechanical toy
questions.explanation: A humorous person 'is like another candle in the room' — a comforting light, (D).

questions.prompt: The author uses the analogy of marionettes to illustrate that humorous people
choices: A) are never forgotten
B) smile frequently
C) control their companions
D) surprise their friends
E) attract many friends
questions.explanation: The marionette 'strings' twitch the mouth-corners up 'at the slightest jest,' i.e. humorous people smile readily — (B). The Master, not the person, does the controlling.

questions.prompt: The author implies that witty people are likely to make remarks that are
choices: A) sentimental
B) emotional
C) displeasing
D) irrelevant
E) explanatory
questions.explanation: 'the mouth of a merely witty person is hard and sour' and the flash 'not always comforting' — witty remarks tend to be displeasing, (C).

questions.prompt: Which of the following best expresses the author's main point?
choices: A) It is more pleasant to be with humorous people than with witty people.
B) Humor is more difficult to achieve than wit.
C) Humorous people make friends easily.
D) Humor and wit are genuine gifts.
E) Witty people are not usually humorous, but humorous people are usually witty.
questions.explanation: 'humor is the more comfortable and livable quality' — the point is that humorous company is pleasanter than witty company, (A).

passages.title: A Portrait of Mr. Duffy
passages.text: Mr. Duffy lived in Chapelizod because he wished to live as far as possible from the city of which he was a citizen and because he found all other suburbs of Dublin mean, modern, and pretentious. He had been for many years cashier of a private bank in Baggot Street. Every morning he came in from Chapelizod by streetcar. At midday he went to Dan Burke's and took his lunch—a bottle of lager beer and a small trayful of arrowroot biscuits. At four o'clock he was set free. He dined in an eating house on George's Street where he felt himself safe from the society of Dublin's gilded youth and where there was a certain plain honesty in the bill of fare. His evenings were spent either before his landlady's piano or roaming about the outskirts of the city. His liking for Mozart's music brought him sometimes to an opera or a concert. These were the only dissipations of his life.

questions.prompt: The author's primary purpose in the passage is to
choices: A) resolve a plot
B) create suspense
C) describe city life
D) describe a scene
E) describe a character
questions.explanation: The whole passage catalogs one man's fixed habits, so it aims to 'describe a character.' D (describe a scene) fails: no single setting is painted; the focus is Duffy's personality and routine.

questions.prompt: Why did Mr. Duffy prefer Chapelizod to the other suburbs of Dublin?
choices: A) It was closer to Dan Burke's.
B) It offered modern conveniences.
C) It was where his business was located.
D) It was a place where he was not known.
E) It was unpretentious and remote from Dublin.
questions.explanation: He 'wished to live as far as possible from the city' and found other suburbs 'mean, modern, and pretentious.' D (unknown) is unsupported; nothing says he sought anonymity.

questions.prompt: Mr. Duffy's chief diversion apparently was
choices: A) work
B) music
C) travel
D) the theater
E) good company
questions.explanation: His 'liking for Mozart's music' and evenings at the piano dominate his leisure. D (theater) fails: opera/concert happen only 'sometimes,' subordinate to his general love of music.

questions.prompt: As he is described in the passage, Mr. Duffy appears to be
choices: A) cruel
B) degenerate
C) a creature of habit
D) a remorseful person
E) a man of many interests
questions.explanation: Every day follows an identical rigid schedule (streetcar, lunch, dinner, piano), marking him a 'creature of habit.' E (many interests) fails: his 'only dissipations' are few and repetitive.

passages.title: Louis XVI and the Colonies
passages.text:
Louis XVI was modest, sincere, and mediocre; he had small capacity for government. He had small, saucer-shaped blue eyes and a very small head, which he finally lost on the guillotine. Americans have always adored Louis XVI as a democratic, great-hearted monarch who sent his armies flocking to our banner that freedom might not perish from the earth.

Louis may have been great-hearted, but he was as undemocratic as the rest of the wigged aristocrats. Democracy was a sharp stone in his shoe, and he bitterly hated all forms of republican government. Not only was he entirely out of sympathy with our desire of independence, he also did all he could to prevent our revolutionary doctrines from being spread in France. He would have liked to see us and our insurgent spirit exterminated. He did not help us out of any unselfish passion for liberty, as Lafayette theatrically insisted he did, but out of pure fear. He was afraid of England. He knew that if England defeated her rebellious colonies—as she probably would unless General Washington had immediate help from some foreign power—her military resources would be tremendous. Then France, Britain's ancient enemy, would meet disaster if the old British lion should choose to pounce.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Louis XVI was greatly concerned about
choices: A) restraining the aristocracy
B) developing French colonies
C) advancing the ideals of freedom
D) protecting France from England
E) winning the admiration of the Americans
questions.explanation: He 'was afraid of England' and feared France would 'meet disaster' if Britain won, so his concern was protecting France. A (restraining aristocracy) contradicts his alliance with aristocrats.

questions.prompt: The author implies that Louis XVI believed that Washington's troops were
choices: A) ignorant and untrained
B) devoted to a worthy cause
C) afraid of French domination
D) unfairly criticized by the British
E) not strong enough to defeat the British
questions.explanation: England 'would' defeat the colonies 'unless...immediate help,' implying the troops were too weak to win alone. B (worthy cause) reflects Americans' view, not Louis's assessment.

questions.prompt: According to the author, Americans have believed that, as a king, Louis XVI was
choices: A) cruel and despotic
B) idealistic but timid
C) sincere but ineffective
D) noble and freedom-loving
E) irresponsible and romantic
questions.explanation: Americans 'adored Louis XVI as a democratic, great-hearted monarch' who championed freedom. C (sincere but ineffective) is the author's own verdict ('modest, sincere, mediocre'), not the American belief.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, France sent aid to General Washington because
choices: A) Lafayette persuaded the king to do so
B) the king wanted to make friends with the Americans
C) the king wanted to express his sympathy for democratic beliefs
D) the king did not want England to increase her military resources
E) influential powers in France threatened to guillotine him if he refused to do so
questions.explanation: He helped 'out of pure fear'—not wanting England's 'military resources' to grow. A (Lafayette persuaded) is what the author explicitly denies as theatrical insistence.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Louis XVI heartily approved of
choices: A) alliance with Britain
B) aiding weaker nations
C) a powerful aristocracy
D) colonial independence
E) republican government
questions.explanation: He was 'as undemocratic as the rest of the wigged aristocrats' and 'hated all forms of republican government,' so he approved a powerful aristocracy. E (republican government) is the opposite of his views.

questions.prompt: The author refers to Lafayette as one who
choices: A) opposed Louis XVI
B) was a wigged aristocrat
C) misrepresented Louis XVI
D) feared the spread of democracy
E) turned Louis XVI against Britain
questions.explanation: Louis aided America not from love of liberty 'as Lafayette theatrically insisted,' so Lafayette misrepresented the king's motives. A (opposed Louis) is unsupported; Lafayette praised him falsely, not opposed him.

passages.title: America's Thirst for Gasoline
passages.text: Approximately 28 percent of all energy used in the United States is devoted to transportation and of that fraction, 40 percent is supplied in the form of gasoline to fuel the nation's nearly 255 million registered passenger vehicles. Americans use more energy to fuel their cars than they do for any other single purpose. The fuel used by American automobiles and personal trucks would just about fill all the energy needs of Japan, a nation of over 127 million and the world's largest consumer of energy after the United States and China. In an urgent effort to reduce consumption of an increasingly costly fuel whose chief reserves lie overseas, the government has rightly identified the American automobile and current habits of its utilization as prime targets for change.

questions.prompt: This passage was probably taken from
choices: A) a historical novel
B) a speech in a play
C) an editorial analysis
D) a gasoline commercial
E) a government contract
questions.explanation: The judgmental phrase 'the government has rightly identified' signals opinion-driven editorial analysis. D (gasoline commercial) fails: the piece urges cutting gasoline use, not selling it.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, Japan's gasoline consumption is
choices: A) equal to that of China
B) 127 million gallons a year
C) equal to that of the United States
D) less than that of the United States
E) larger than that of any other country
questions.explanation: US car fuel alone would fill all Japan's needs, and Japan ranks as consumer only 'after the United States and China,' so Japan uses less than the US. C (equal to US) directly contradicts this.

questions.prompt: This passage is mostly about
choices: A) energy needs of Japan
B) alternative energy sources
C) car sales in the United States
D) the extent of oil reserves overseas
E) gasoline consumption in the United States
questions.explanation: The passage centers on American gasoline use for cars as the top energy purpose. A (Japan's energy) is only a comparison point, not the subject.

questions.prompt: The passage provides information to answer which of the following questions?
choices: A) How can current driving habits in the United States be changed?
B) How many Chinese have automobiles or personal trucks?
C) What proportion of United States energy is used for transportation?
D) How are various countries meeting their energy needs?
E) When will the world's oil supply run out?
questions.explanation: The text states '28 percent of all energy...devoted to transportation,' answering this directly. A (how to change habits) is named as a goal but never explained, so it is not answered.

passages.title: The Hunting Camp
passages.text: We had been five days in the hunting camp, and the meat, which had all this time been drying in the sun, was now fit for transportation. Sufficient buffalo hides also had been obtained to make the next season's lodges, but it remained to provide the long poles on which they were to be supported. These were only to be had among the tall spruce woods in the hills, and in that direction, therefore, our next move was to be made. Amid the general abundance of the camp at this time, there were no instances of individual want; for, although the hide and tongue of the buffalo belong by exclusive right to the hunter who has killed it, anyone else is equally entitled to help himself from the rest of the carcass. Thus the weak, the aged, and even the lazy come in for a share of the spoils, and many a helpless old woman, who would otherwise perish from starvation, is sustained in abundance.

questions.prompt: The main reason for staying five days in the hunting camp was to
choices: A) allow the meat to dry
B) obtain spruce poles
C) secure additional buffalo hides
D) provide for the aged and helpless
E) eat as much of the fresh meat as possible
questions.explanation: The meat 'had all this time been drying in the sun' and was now 'fit for transportation,' the purpose of the stay. C (hides) is secondary—merely 'also' obtained, not the main reason.

questions.prompt: The hunter who killed a buffalo
choices: A) was entitled to the better cuts of meat
B) could do anything he wished with the carcass
C) shared the carcass only with his elderly relatives
D) could take only the hide and tongue before sharing the carcass
E) usually gave the carcass to the old and infirm and to the sick
questions.explanation: Only 'the hide and tongue' belong exclusively to the hunter; 'anyone else is equally entitled' to the rest. B (do anything with carcass) fails since the carcass is communally shared.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the buffalo hides were to be used in making
choices: A) canoes
B) clothing
C) blankets
D) dwellings
E) moccasins
questions.explanation: Hides were obtained 'to make the next season's lodges,' i.e., dwellings. B (clothing) is unsupported; the text specifies lodges, not garments.

questions.prompt: Which of the following conclusions can best be drawn from the passage?
choices: A) There was no permanent settlement and the entire tribe was at the hunting camp.
B) There was no permanent settlement, but there were several separate camps that moved along together.
C) There was no permanent settlement and the entire tribe, with the exception of the weak and the aged, was at the hunting camp.
D) While the hunters were at the hunting camp, the rest of the tribe remained in a permanent settlement up in the hills.
E) While the hunters were at the hunting camp, the rest of the tribe remained at a permanent settlement at a location not given.
questions.explanation: The weak, aged, and lazy share spoils at the camp, implying no separate settlement and the whole tribe present. C fails: it excludes the weak and aged, who are explicitly there being 'sustained.'

passages.title: Our Neighbor the Moon
passages.text:
As earth whirls along its endless journey through space, it has a companion that is always beside it—the moon. The moon is a small planet. It is only about one-fourth as big as the earth.

The moon is our nearest neighbor in space. The stars are billions of miles away. The sun is millions of miles away. But the moon is only about 239,000 miles away. That makes the moon truly a next-door neighbor.

In a way, the moon "belongs" to the earth. Just as earth moves around the sun, the moon moves around the earth. It is held in place by the tug of earth's stronger gravity. A planet that is held by another planet this way is called a satellite. The moon is earth's satellite.

The moon is a ball of gray rock, some of which is covered with dust. It has no air or water—and, of course, no plants or animals. Its whole surface is nothing but mountains and plains of rock. When we look up at a full moon, we often see dark patches. These dark patches are the lowlands. They seem to form a shadowy face that people have named "the man in the moon." The brighter parts of the moon are the highlands.

In ancient times, many people worshipped the moon. The Romans, who thought the moon was a goddess, named it Luna. Our word lunar means "of the moon."

questions.prompt: Why does the author think that the moon "belongs" (line 7) to the earth?
choices: A) The moon could not exist without the earth.
B) One can see the man in the moon from earth.
C) Ancient Romans considered it a goddess.
D) It is earth's nearest neighbor.
E) It is a satellite of the earth.
questions.explanation: The moon 'belongs' because it orbits earth held by gravity—'a planet held by another...is called a satellite.' D (nearest neighbor) is a separate fact, not the reason for belonging.

questions.prompt: Which of the following can be found on the moon?
choices: A) air
B) water
C) plants
D) animals
E) mountains
questions.explanation: The surface is 'nothing but mountains and plains of rock,' with 'no air or water...no plants or animals.' Thus only mountains qualify; A-D are all explicitly absent.

questions.prompt: According to the author, which of the following most accounts for the "man in the moon?"
choices: A) a myth
B) the lowlands
C) a goddess
D) the Romans
E) the highlands
questions.explanation: The 'dark patches are the lowlands' that 'seem to form a shadowy face' named the man in the moon. E (highlands) are the brighter parts, not the dark face.

questions.prompt: The author's main purpose for writing the passage is most likely to
choices: A) inform the reader about the moon
B) dispel myths about the man in the moon
C) describe the origin of the word "lunar"
D) compare the moon and the earth
E) explain why the ancient Romans worshipped the moon
questions.explanation: The passage gives broad factual descriptions of the moon's size, distance, and surface—to inform. B (dispel myths) is too narrow; the man-in-the-moon is only one detail among many facts.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, which of the following statements is NOT true?
choices: A) The moon is a planet.
B) The moon is a satellite.
C) The moon's lowlands appear dark from the earth.
D) The moon is four times larger than earth.
E) The moon is closer to the earth than anything else in space.
questions.explanation: The moon is 'one-fourth as big as the earth,' so 'four times larger than earth' is false and thus NOT true. A (a planet) is stated true in the text ('a small planet').

passages.title: A Powerful Rocket Fuel
passages.text:
Dinitrogen tetroxide is a chemical compound made up of nitrogen and oxygen. It is abbreviated N2O4 and is often simply called nitrogen tetroxide or NTO. Propelling rockets is one of the best examples of the use to which nitrogen tetroxide can be put. It is often combined with a hydrazine-based rocket fuel. Because it burns on contact without needing a separate source to ignite it, it is one of the most important rocket propellants ever developed.

By the late 1950s, NTO was the storable oxidizer of choice for U.S. and Soviet rockets. It was used on the space shuttle, and continues to be used on most geo-stationary satellites and many deep-space probes. However, its use is not without risk. NTO poisoning nearly killed three astronauts when dangerous fumes were vented inside their cabin by mistake.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that nitrogen tetroxide
choices: A) is expensive
B) must be burned
C) requires a license for use
D) is vital to the space program
E) should not be combined with any other substance
questions.explanation: Called "one of the most important rocket propellants," used on the shuttle, satellites, and deep-space probes—so it's vital to the space program. B ("must be burned") misreads its self-igniting property.

questions.prompt: Which of the following titles best describes the content of the passage?
choices: A) Fire Prevention in Space
B) A Chemical Experiment
C) Why Nitrogen Tetroxide Is No Longer Used
D) Nitrogen Tetroxide and the Soviets
E) An Introduction to Nitrogen Tetroxide
questions.explanation: The passage broadly introduces what NTO is, its uses, and its risks. C ("No Longer Used") is wrong since it "continues to be used"; other titles cover only a detail.

questions.prompt: The author suggests that nitrogen tetroxide
choices: A) is an illegal compound
B) needs to be lit with fire
C) must be carefully vented
D) should be forbidden in space
E) is only used for propelling rockets
questions.explanation: Fumes "vented inside their cabin by mistake" nearly killed astronauts, implying it must be carefully vented. B contradicts that it "burns on contact without needing a separate source."

questions.prompt: The author uses the phrase "burns on contact" (line 4) to show that nitrogen tetroxide is
choices: A) very hot
B) flammable
C) useful in space
D) used only with rocket fuel
E) too dangerous to use in industry
questions.explanation: "Burns on contact without needing a separate source to ignite it" shows it is flammable. A ("very hot") isn't what ignition-on-contact demonstrates.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, nitrogen tetroxide
choices: A) cannot be stored
B) has limited power
C) is unusable at high altitudes
D) is an important rocket propellant
E) is not suitable for use in deep space
questions.explanation: Explicitly "one of the most important rocket propellants." A ("cannot be stored") contradicts "the storable oxidizer of choice."

questions.prompt: The author's tone in the second paragraph can best be described as
choices: A) bored
B) serious
C) annoyed
D) humorous
E) enthusiastic
questions.explanation: The second paragraph reports adoption and a near-fatal poisoning—a serious, factual tone. E (enthusiastic) better fits paragraph one; nothing is humorous or annoyed.

passages.title: Green Politicians
passages.text:
Washington is full of green politicians supporting causes that other people demand they support. Indeed, this truism of human psychology is even truer of many so-called seasoned politicians. Like certain senators, many representatives, and all of the lobbyists, they portray themselves as VIPs, taking uncontroversial stances that capture the public's interest and pay off in the currency of fame.

But there is another, all too rare kind of politician: the authentic professional who steeps himself or herself in the art of public service with dazzling skill and persuasiveness. What marks these politicians, besides integrity, is their drive to achieve impressive results rather than fame, despite all the temptations of publicity and self-indulgence. Most began their careers in local government, and for many, helping the little guy remains their top priority, despite the temptation to overlook the powerless. Above all, they seek to pose the unanswered questions—to make an honorable mark on an often false town.

questions.prompt: The author is primarily concerned with
choices: A) defining politics
B) criticizing artificial politicians
C) praising politicians
D) describing the historical role of the public servant
E) considering the contributions politicians can make to society
questions.explanation: The piece builds to admire "the authentic professional" politician of integrity and results. B covers only paragraph one; E ("contributions to society") isn't the characterizing focus.

questions.prompt: The author admires politicians who
choices: A) enact meaningful change
B) get a great deal of publicity
C) overlook the local residents
D) know how to enjoy themselves
E) support only the most popular positions
questions.explanation: Their "drive to achieve impressive results rather than fame" means enacting meaningful change. B (publicity) is what the admired kind rejects.

questions.prompt: The author would probably have the greatest respect for a politician who
choices: A) tackles meaningful issues
B) attains international fame
C) knows how to enjoy himself
D) actively seeks the political limelight
E) knows how to tell people what they want to hear
questions.explanation: Greatest respect goes to those who "pose the unanswered questions" and "achieve impressive results"—tackling meaningful issues. Fame/limelight options are what he criticizes.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward politicians who seek fame may best be described as
choices: A) sympathetic
B) indifferent
C) respectful
D) amused
E) critical
questions.explanation: Fame-seekers are "green," taking "uncontroversial stances" that "pay off in the currency of fame"—a critical view, not sympathetic or respectful.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the author most likely to discuss next?
choices: A) how to relax and be yourself
B) the glamour of a political career
C) what makes certain politicians famous
D) the careers of some exceptional politicians
E) some of the most popular politicians today
questions.explanation: Having praised this rare type ("most began in local government"), the author would next give concrete examples—careers of exceptional politicians. Other options revisit the criticized fame theme.

passages.title: The National Black Theatre
passages.text:
The National Black Theatre in Harlem is one of the great successes of our time. The survival of the institution and the way it has found a secure place in an increasingly crowded theater world are considerable accomplishments. Of greater significance, though, are the consistently high level of the institution's performances and the unfailing enthusiasm of each actor. Of all the theater groups that regularly appear in New York, it shows the least danger of succumbing to routine.

Dr. Barbara Ann Teer's National Black Theatre is animated by a sense of purpose that is largely moral in nature. In the midst of the despair that followed the 1968 assassination of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., Teer was determined to give American-born actors of African descent a place on the stage. She sought to help other African-Americans so that they would not have to face the difficulties she had faced.

Nobody was better qualified than Teer to understand what black actors could do if given the incentive and the opportunity, and time has proved her faith well-founded. But Teer was too shrewd an artistic administrator and too serious an artist not to know that the only standard she and her institution would in the long run be guided by is artistic. As she said in an interview, "We must begin building cultural centers where we can enjoy being free, open and black, where we can find out how talented we really are." That day, so far as I am concerned, has already arrived.

questions.prompt: The author's primary purpose is to
choices: A) describe how a theater institution is formed
B) discuss the success of one theater institution
C) contrast the artistic qualities of various theater institutions
D) encourage the establishment of more African American theater institutions
E) clarify the motives underlying the formation of theater institutions
questions.explanation: Opens by calling it "one of the great successes" and details that success. D (encourage more institutions) and E (motives) aren't the focus; it centers on this one theater.

questions.prompt: Without changing the author's meaning, "secure" (line 2) could be replaced by
choices: A) overconfident
B) profitable
C) shielded
D) assured
E) orderly
questions.explanation: A "secure place" in the theater world means an assured/established one. C ("shielded") and B ("profitable") don't fit "found a secure place."

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward National Black Theatre can best be described as one of
choices: A) cautious optimism
B) impulsive criticism
C) enthusiastic approval
D) theoretical justification
E) perplexed bewilderment
questions.explanation: "Great successes," "consistently high level," "that day...has already arrived"—enthusiastic approval, not merely cautious optimism (A).

questions.prompt: The author implies which of the following about theater institutions?
choices: A) At present it is not possible to form new theater institutions.
B) Not all theater institutions survive against their competitors.
C) Most theater institutions regularly appear in New York City.
D) Most theater institutions maintain high levels of performance.
E) The best actors do not remain with the institutions that gave them their start.
questions.explanation: Calling survival in "an increasingly crowded theater world" a "considerable accomplishment" implies not all institutions survive competitors. D (most keep high standards) contradicts the praise of this one's rare quality.

questions.prompt: Teer's attitude toward the artistic standards that should be used in judging the National Black Theatre is
choices: A) unselfish
B) objective
C) simplistic
D) regrettable
E) noncommittal
questions.explanation: "The only standard...guided by is artistic"—she judges by artistic merit, an objective yardstick. Unselfish/regrettable/noncommittal don't match her firm, merit-based stance.

passages.title: A Vaccine for Pneumonia
passages.text:
In the late 1970s, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved the first vaccine that successfully prevented pneumococcal pneumonia. Researchers found that the rate that pneumonia occurs increases as we get older. In 2012, the FDA approved a new vaccine for adults 50 and older. This vaccine protects against thirteen strains of pneumococcal bacteria, which cause meningitis, pneumonia, and ear infections. The FDA said that 300,000 adults 50 years and older are hospitalized every year for pneumococcal pneumonia. Initial distribution was being aimed at persons over 65.

"Despite the wide use of antibiotics, pneumonia today is the sixth leading cause of death in the United States," an FDA representative said. "The type of pneumonia against which the vaccine protects accounts for a major portion of these deaths. The vaccine is effective in at least 80 percent of the people who receive it." Still, all these years later, the vaccine is a safe and effective way to prevent pneumonia.

questions.prompt: This passage is primarily about
choices: A) the first vaccine invented
B) leading causes of death
C) a vaccine for pneumonia
D) illness in the United States
E) the Food and Drug Administration
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the pneumococcal pneumonia vaccine, its approval and effectiveness. E (the FDA) and B (causes of death) are supporting details.

questions.prompt: From the passage, it can be concluded that the vaccine approved in the late 1970s probably
choices: A) increased the authority of the FDA
B) made the use of antibiotics obsolete
C) reduced the number of deaths from pneumonia
D) provided protection against illnesses other than pneumonia
E) raised health expenses for people in the United States
questions.explanation: The 1970s vaccine "successfully prevented pneumococcal pneumonia," so it likely reduced pneumonia deaths. D (other illnesses) describes the later 2012 vaccine, not the 1970s one.

questions.prompt: Which of the following could be substituted for "occurs" (line 3) without changing the meaning of the sentence?
choices: A) falls out
B) breaks in
C) comes down
D) bursts forth
E) presents itself
questions.explanation: "The rate that pneumonia occurs" means the rate it presents itself/appears. Idioms like "comes down" or "breaks in" don't fit the rate-of-occurrence sense.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, pneumococcal bacteria cause all of the following EXCEPT
choices: A) pneumonia
B) meningitis
C) infections
D) influenza
E) hospitalizations
questions.explanation: Bacteria "cause meningitis, pneumonia, and ear infections"—influenza is never listed. A, B, C are all named.

questions.prompt: The style of the passage is most like that found in
choices: A) a textbook
B) an almanac
C) a news article
D) an encyclopedia
E) an advertisement
questions.explanation: It reports an approval and quotes an "FDA representative," reading like a news article. A textbook/encyclopedia wouldn't quote a spokesperson this way.

passages.title: No Drearier View
passages.text:
In the whole world there can be no more dreary view than that from the northern slope of the Sierra Blanco. As far as the eye can reach stretches the great flat plain-land, all dusted over with patches of alkali, and intersected by clumps of the dwarfish chaparral bushes. On the extreme verge of the horizon lie a long chain of mountain peaks, with their rugged summits flecked with snow. In this great stretch of country there is no sign of life, nor of anything appertaining to life. There is no bird in the steel-blue heaven, no movement upon the dull, grey earth—above all, there is absolute silence. Listen as one may, there is no shadow of a sound in all that mighty wilderness; nothing but silence—complete and heart-subduing silence.

It has been said there is nothing appertaining to life upon the broad plain. That is hardly true. Looking down from the Sierra Blanco, one sees a pathway traced out across the desert, which winds away and is lost in the extreme distance. It is rutted with wheels and trodden down by the feet of many adventurers. Here and there are scattered white objects which glisten in the sun, and stand out against the dull deposit of alkali.

Approach, and examine them! They are bones: some large and coarse, others smaller and more delicate. The former have belonged to oxen, and the latter to men. For fifteen hundred miles one may trace this ghastly caravan route by these scattered remains of those who had fallen by the wayside.

questions.prompt: The passage suggests that travelers died as the result of
choices: A) enemy attack
B) violent storms
C) wild animals
D) disease or pestilence
E) heat and lack of water
questions.explanation: A waterless, alkali "desert" under a "merciless sun" strewn with bones implies death from heat and lack of water. No enemies, storms, or animals are mentioned.

questions.prompt: The word “rutted” (line 12) means
choices: A) ditched
B) gated
C) mated
D) closed
E) furrowed
questions.explanation: "Rutted with wheels and trodden down" describes a path grooved/furrowed by traffic. "Ditched" and "gated" don't fit wheel ruts.

questions.prompt: In the second paragraph, the tone of the author is best described as
choices: A) angry
B) ironic
C) excited
D) indifferent
E) surprised
questions.explanation: After claiming life exists, the author reveals the "life" is actually bones of the dead—an ironic reversal. Not angry, excited, or indifferent.

questions.prompt: The most important function of the second paragraph is to emphasize that
choices: A) this is a desert wilderness
B) life exists on the Sierra Blanco plain
C) many lives were lost crossing this desert
D) only the bravest explorers should traverse this area
E) carving roads through desert is dangerous work
questions.explanation: The paragraph's payoff is the "ghastly caravan route" of scattered human/ox bones—emphasizing many died crossing. B ("life exists") is the ironic setup, not the real function.

questions.prompt: The author of this passage is primarily concerned with
choices: A) retracing the path of previous explorers
B) telling the story of an ill-fated caravan journey
C) emphasizing the bleakness of this desolate landscape
D) proving life can be found on the Sierra Blanco plain
E) describing the effects of alkali on bushes and bones
questions.explanation: The whole passage dwells on "dreary," silent, lifeless desolation. D (proving life exists) is used only ironically to heighten the bleakness.

passages.title: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud
passages.text:
I wandered lonely as a cloud
That floats on high o'er vales and hills,
When all at once I saw a crowd,
A host, of golden daffodils,
Beside the lake, beneath the trees,
Fluttering and dancing in the breeze.

Continuous as the stars that shine
And twinkle on the Milky Way.
They stretched in never-ending line
Along the margin of a bay.
Ten thousand saw I at a glance,
Tossing their heads in sprightly dance.

The waves beside them danced, but they
Out-did the sparkling waves in glee.
A poet could not but be gay,
In such a jocund company.
I gazed—and gazed—but little thought
What wealth the show to me had brought.

For oft, when on my couch I lie
In vacant or in pensive mood,
They flash upon that inward eye
Which is the bliss of solitude,
And then my heart with pleasure fills,
And dances with the daffodils.

questions.prompt: The poet uses which technique to describe the daffodils?
choices: A) parable
B) irony
C) persuasion
D) alliteration
E) personification
questions.explanation: Daffodils 'tossing their heads' and 'dancing' are given human action, i.e. personification. Alliteration (D) appears incidentally but isn't the technique used to describe them.

questions.prompt: Which of the following words could be substituted for "sprightly" (line 12)?
choices: A) weary
B) irksome
C) energetic
D) ambitious
E) interesting
questions.explanation: 'Sprightly dance' conveys lively motion, so 'energetic' fits. 'Weary' and 'irksome' are opposite in spirit.

questions.prompt: "Jocund" (line 16) most nearly means
choices: A) fancy
B) cheerful
C) touching
D) humorless
E) heartbroken
questions.explanation: 'Jocund company' beside a gleeful poet means 'cheerful.' 'Humorless' and 'heartbroken' contradict the joyful tone.

questions.prompt: The tone of the last stanza can be described as both
choices: A) wistful and happy
B) childish and candid
C) proud and indignant
D) confident and comical
E) sarcastic and despairing
questions.explanation: The 'pensive mood' of solitude turns to a heart that 'with pleasure fills' — wistful yet happy. Nothing proud, sarcastic, or childish appears.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the main idea of the poem?
choices: A) You should always be envious of the beauty of nature.
B) Flowers can often remind you of unpleasant past events.
C) Ocean waves can remind you of flowers moving with a breeze.
D) Gazing at the Milky Way can never replace the joy that flowers can bring.
E) Nature provides so much peace and joy that even the memory of nature can make you happy.
questions.explanation: The remembered daffodils later 'flash upon that inward eye,' bringing pleasure — nature's joy persists in memory. Other options misstate the poem's positive message.

passages.title: Before the Battle
passages.text: Through the noonday heat they waited, their energy draining away bit by bit under the merciless sun. As the hours went by, Daniel's foreboding deepened. This waiting was not the same as the times he had crouched behind a rock eager for Raymond's signal. It was no flimsily-guarded caravan they awaited, but a group of real soldiers. And behind him was no tight-knit band that would move with precision and cunning, only a cluster of untried boys. Even now, as he glanced up, the flutter of a coat sleeve betrayed one of them. Still, he could count on them. He knew that every boy in the band was prepared to give his life. It was up to him, the one they had chosen leader, to see that none of them had to.

questions.prompt: At the beginning of the passage, Daniel's attitude is one of deep
choices: A) pride
B) sorrow
C) disgust
D) ignorance
E) uneasiness
questions.explanation: 'His foreboding deepened' signals uneasiness. Not pride or sorrow; the dominant feeling is apprehensive dread before the fight.

questions.prompt: The effect of the sun on the waiting boys was to
choices: A) give them courage
B) drain their energy
C) make them optimistic
D) make them livelier but more frightened of their task
E) confuse them and make them believe that Raymond was signaling
questions.explanation: 'Energy draining away...under the merciless sun' directly states the sun drained their energy. Livelier/optimistic options reverse this.

questions.prompt: The author mentions that "the flutter of a coat sleeve betrayed one of them" (lines 6-7) to show that Daniel's boys were
choices: A) inexperienced
B) dishonest
C) patriotic
D) angry
E) hurried
questions.explanation: 'Untried boys' whose careless coat-sleeve flutter gives them away shows inexperience. Not dishonesty or patriotism — the detail marks their rawness.

questions.prompt: The fact that Daniel knew that every boy in the band was prepared to give his life made him realize that he
choices: A) was an unpopular leader
B) lacked sympathy for the band
C) was reluctant to give his own life
D) felt that attacking the soldiers was cruel
E) carried a great responsibility because he was their leader
questions.explanation: Knowing all were ready to die, 'it was up to him...to see that none of them had to' — his burden as chosen leader. He isn't unpopular or unsympathetic.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the mood created by the author in the passage?
choices: A) anxious suspense
B) intense activity
C) boredom
D) optimism
E) grief
questions.explanation: Tense waiting, deepening foreboding, and untried boys create anxious suspense. There's no activity, boredom, or grief — only dread of what's coming.

passages.title: My Grandmother's Theater
passages.text:
In China my grandmother loved the theater (which I, having been brought up in America, would not have been able to understand because of my limited vocabulary). When the actors came to the village and set up their scaffolding, my grandmother bought a large space up front. She would stay days and nights sleeping under the stars. Unfortunately, there was danger that bandits, who followed the actors, would make raids on households thinned out during performances.

"But, Grandmother," the family complained, "the bandits will steal the tables while we're gone." The family took the chairs to plays.

"I want every last one of you at that theater. I don't want to watch that play by myself. How can I laugh all by myself? You want me to clap alone, is that it? I want everybody there."

"The robbers will ransack the food."

"So let them. Cook up the food and take it to the theater. If you're so worried about bandits, leave the doors open. Leave the windows open. Leave the house wide open. We are going to the theater without worries."

So they left the doors open, and my whole family went to watch the actors. Sure enough, that night the bandits struck—not the house, but the theater. "Bandits!" the audience screamed. "Bandits!" the actors screamed. My family ran in all directions. When my grandmother and mother made their way home, the rest of the family was home safe, proof to my grandmother that our family was immune to harm as long as they went to plays. They went to many plays after that.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the narrator does not
choices: A) fear bandits
B) like school
C) like to cook
D) like to go to the theater
E) understand Chinese very well
questions.explanation: Narrator 'would not have been able to understand because of my limited vocabulary' — doesn't understand Chinese well. The narrator does fear nothing about theater dislike; text specifies the language gap.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that the plays were presented in
choices: A) the home of one of the actors
B) a remote part of the village
C) the narrator's home
D) an outdoor area
E) the village school
questions.explanation: Actors 'set up their scaffolding' and grandmother slept 'under the stars' — an outdoor venue. Not a home or school.

questions.prompt: The "scaffolding" mentioned in line 3 can best be interpreted to mean
choices: A) a temporary stage
B) seats for the playgoers
C) dressing rooms for the actors
D) the framework of a building
E) partitions for the cast to wait behind
questions.explanation: Actors 'set up their scaffolding' to perform on — a temporary stage. Not a permanent building's framework or dressing rooms.

questions.prompt: The family most likely took their chairs to the theater because
choices: A) Grandmother wanted the chairs with her
B) the chairs were the family's most valuable possession
C) the theater had no seats
D) the bandits could not then steal them
E) the chairs were worth more than the tables
questions.explanation: Grandmother bought only 'a large space up front' with no seating, so chairs were needed to sit. She's dismissive of theft, so protecting chairs (D) isn't the motive.

questions.prompt: Grandmother's attitude toward the bandits can best be described as
choices: A) nonchalant
B) indulgent
C) inquisitive
D) considerate
E) intolerant
questions.explanation: 'So let them...Leave the house wide open. We are going to the theater without worries' shows nonchalance. Not intolerant or considerate toward the bandits.

passages.title: The Feminist Crusade
passages.text: What really launched the nineteenth-century feminist crusade was the desire on the part of women to participate in the movement for the abolition of slavery. The refusal to admit women to the existing antislavery societies or even to permit them to speak in public for the cause led to defiance on the part of such women as Lucretia Mott. Barred from existing organizations, women abolitionists formed a national organization of their own in 1834. So great was the opposition that in 1838 a mob burned the hall in which the women were meeting. The next year the issue of admitting women to the national antislavery society of men broke up the organization into two movements, one composed of men alone, and one in which women cooperated with men on equal terms.

questions.prompt: The main idea of the passage is that
choices: A) women formed some of the earliest antislavery societies
B) the abolitionist movement broke up into two separate movements
C) men were violently opposed to the nineteenth-century feminists
D) the desire of women to fight slavery led to the nineteenth-century feminist movement
E) the nineteenth-century feminists cooperated with men to oppose slavery
questions.explanation: Passage opens: women's desire to join abolition 'launched the nineteenth-century feminist crusade.' The split (B) is a detail, not the central point.

questions.prompt: The style of the passage is most like that found in a
choices: A) novel
B) diary
C) biography
D) history text
E) short story
questions.explanation: Objective recounting of dated events (1834, 1838, 1839) reads like a history text. Not a personal diary or fictional story.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "crusade" (line 1) most nearly means
choices: A) career
B) caucus
C) campaign
D) ceremony
E) convention
questions.explanation: 'Feminist crusade' = a sustained movement, i.e. campaign. 'Caucus' and 'convention' are single gatherings, not the movement itself.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, which of the following took place in 1839?
choices: A) Lucretia Mott was not allowed to speak in public.
B) A mob burned the hall in which the women abolitionists were meeting.
C) The men's antislavery society split into two groups.
D) Women formed their own antislavery society.
E) Women refused to participate in the men's antislavery society.
questions.explanation: 'The next year' (1839) 'broke up the organization into two movements' — the men's society split. The mob-burning and founding occurred in 1838 and 1834.

passages.title: Elephants in Battle
passages.text: The elephants added to the difficulty of the march. But they had an important role to play in Hannibal's campaign. Elephants were the tanks of ancient warfare. Ever since they had first been encountered in battle by Alexander's troops on the banks of the Hydaspes, their value as a substitute for war chariots has been recognized. They were extremely valuable pack animals and, although their behavior in battle was unpredictable, they could be useful against enemy calvary that had not been trained to meet them. But, above all, they were a powerful psychological weapon. Hannibal knew that his elephants would create panic among the Roman soldiers, who would never have seen such creatures before.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, elephants served all of the purposes in warfare EXCEPT:
choices: A) Behaving in a reliable manner
B) Fighting in battle
C) Confusing the enemy
D) Scaring the enemy
E) Transporting supplies
questions.explanation: 'Their behavior in battle was unpredictable' — so reliable behavior is NOT a purpose served. Fighting, scaring, confusing, and transporting are all cited.

questions.prompt:
Which of the following statements describes the author's opinion of using the elephants in battle?

I. It was often not worth the risk because their behavior is unpredictable and they make the march more difficult.
II. There were many advantages to using elephants in warfare.
III. The most important use of the elephants was as pack animals.
choices: A) I only
B) II only
C) III only
D) II and III
E) I, II and III
questions.explanation: Author stresses elephants' value (tanks, pack animals, psychological weapon) — II only. I (not worth risk) isn't the author's view; III is wrong since the psychological role mattered 'above all.'

questions.prompt: According to the passage, who was Hannibal fighting?
choices: A) Alexander
B) Elephants
C) Hydaspes
D) Romans
E) This information is not stated in the passage
questions.explanation: Elephants 'would create panic among the Roman soldiers' — Hannibal fought Romans. Alexander and Hydaspes are historical references, not his foes.

questions.prompt: For what reason are the elephants described as a "powerful psychological weapon"?
choices: A) They are very large and intimidating.
B) They were unfamiliar to the enemy.
C) They were unpredictable.
D) They gave Hannibal's troops confidence.
E) Hannibal's troops could hide behind them.
questions.explanation: Romans 'would never have seen such creatures before,' so their unfamiliarity made elephants a psychological weapon. Size (A) isn't the stated reason.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes Hannibal's intent in using elephants against his enemies?
choices: A) provoke anger
B) create surprise
C) inspire confidence
D) exploit weakness
E) undermine positions
questions.explanation: Facing creatures they'd never seen, Romans would panic — Hannibal aimed to create surprise/shock. Not to provoke anger or inspire his own troops' confidence.

passages.title: The Art of Dowsing
passages.text:
Moses may be said to have been the first dowser when he brought forth water by striking a rock with his rod. Outside of this biblical story, the first historical reference to dowsers is medieval. Martin Luther declared that dowsing was a sin, and there is a detailed account from the 1550s of German miners who used wooden sticks to locate minerals and ores.

Today, thousands of people still practice dowsing, following a method that is not much different from the one employed by those medieval miners. These dowsers employ a diving rod or some other implement to locate water, metal ores, or other valuables hidden beneath the earth's surface. The oldest and most commonly used dividing rod is a forked or loop-shaped rod made of wood. This rod is held firmly in the palms of the dowser's hands, and any sudden swing, either upward or downward, is taken as a signal of the presence of underground water, metal deposits, or even buried treasure. There are still questions as to whether or not dowsing actually works, but there is no question that it is a popular practice throughout the world.

Skeptics point out that studies have failed to validate dowsing or to provide any scientific basis for the practice. Dowsers, for instance, claim that the dowsing rod is animated by the presence of an underground water or mineral source. But careful observation has shown that the agitation of the instrument is due to slight muscular movements of the dowser, which may be subconscious, but are certainly not "magical."

Dowsers also feel that their talent to locate hidden objects is a special one, a psychic gift. Scientists sympathetic to their cause say that the dowsers are really responding to electromagnetic currents beneath the earth's surface, which indicate the presence of minerals or water. Skeptics have a different explanation. They note that dowsers usually work on land that they know well and are familiar with all the signs, nature's visual clues, that suggest the presence of water in an area.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the author's attitude toward dowsing?
choices: A) hostile
B) amused
C) positive
D) enthusiastic
E) dubious
questions.explanation: Author repeatedly raises 'questions as to whether or not dowsing works' and foregrounds skeptics' explanations, staying doubtful. Not hostile—he presents both sides evenhandedly, so 'dubious' fits better than 'hostile.'

questions.prompt: According to the passage, dowsing may involve all of the following EXCEPT:
choices: A) psychic gift
B) electromagnetic currents
C) magic
D) popular practice
E) visual clues
questions.explanation: Rod movement is 'certainly not magical,' so magic is excluded. Psychic gift, electromagnetic currents, popular practice, and visual clues are all explicitly mentioned as involved.

questions.prompt: The objective of the dowser is to
choices: A) strike rocks.
B) discover underground matter.
C) perform magic.
D) find wood that has special location properties.
E) pick up signals from water and minerals.
questions.explanation: Dowsers use rods 'to locate water, metal ores, or other valuables hidden beneath the earth's surface.' E ('pick up signals') describes a disputed claimed mechanism, not the actual objective.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes a dowser?
choices: A) Scientist
B) Mystic
C) Skeptic
D) Explorer
E) Sinner
questions.explanation: Dowsers 'feel that their talent...is a special one, a psychic gift,' marking them as mystics. 'Sinner' (Luther's label) and 'skeptic'/'scientist' describe others, not the dowser.

questions.prompt: It is most reasonable to infer from the passage that
choices: A) dowsing is an anti-religious practice.
B) dowsing is somewhat successful since it continues to be used.
C) dowsers are itinerant practitioners.
D) wood has special locational properties.
