E) underground water sends electromagnetic signals.
questions.explanation: Passage stresses thousands still practice and it is popular worldwide despite doubts, implying some perceived success sustains it. E (electromagnetic signals) is only a contested sympathetic claim, not a safe inference.

passages.title: The Betsy Ross Legend
passages.text:
Several legends surround the first American flag. Perhaps the best known and most widely accepted is that of Betsy Ross. Legend tells of George Washington, George Ross and Robert Morris being appointed in June 1776 to design a national flag. Supposedly, Washington made a crude design that was dispatched to Betsy Ross, wife of George Ross's nephew and an expert seamstress, in Philadelphia. Taking the rough sketch and changing only the number of points on the stars from six to five, Mrs. Ross cut and sewed the first national flag for the United States.

History tells us Betsy Ross was a twice-widowed seamstress in Philadelphia who may have made some navy flags. While it is documented that Washington was in Philadelphia for two weeks in 1776, the trip was made on urgent military business. It is doubtful he would have had time to design a flag and, in fact, there is no evidence of his being ordered to do so. Also, the Betsy Ross story was first told in 1870, 94 years after it supposedly took place. The story has all the proper ingredients--hurried meetings, hasty sketches, patriotic widow sewing the new nation's flag for the country her late husband died defending. However, history has proven it to be just a story.

The origins of the Stars and Stripes are shrouded in mystery, legends and half-truths. Facts strongly suggest that the flag had no single designer but was a conglomeration of ideas and designs.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the best title for the selection?
choices: A) Legends Surrounding the First Official Flag
B) Debunking the Betsy Ross Story
C) The Stars and Stripes
D) Navy Flags to Nation's Flag
E) Flag Maker of the United States
questions.explanation: The passage details the Betsy Ross legend then dismantles it as 'just a story.' Only one legend is actually developed, so 'Debunking the Betsy Ross Story' fits better than the broader 'Legends.'

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the author's opinion of Betsy Ross?
choices: A) A fraud
B) A hero
C) Talented
D) Victimized
E) Unskilled
questions.explanation: Ross is called an 'expert seamstress' who 'may have made some navy flags'; author faults the legend, not her. 'Fraud' misreads it—the story is false but Ross isn't portrayed as deceitful.

questions.prompt: The author suggests that the flag was probably designed by
choices: A) George Washington, George Ross and Robert Morris
B) Betsy Ross
C) Betsy Ross's deceased husband
D) Several unknown people
E) A group of seamstresses in Philadelphia
questions.explanation: Closing line: the flag 'had no single designer but was a conglomeration of ideas and designs,' i.e., several unknown people—not Washington's committee or Ross.

questions.prompt: What does the author mean when she says, "The story has all the proper ingredients?"
choices: A) All of the facts support her theory.
B) The story is believable.
C) The story has been told in a way that makes people want to believe it.
D) The story has a beginning, a middle and an end.
E) A recipe for a myth is in the story.
questions.explanation: The listed 'ingredients' (hurried meetings, patriotic widow) are dramatic, appealing details that make the myth attractive, hence 'told in a way that makes people want to believe it.' E is mere wordplay on 'recipe.'

passages.title: Rikki-tikki-tavi
passages.text:
This is the story of the great war that Rikki-tikki-tavi fought single-handed, through the bathrooms of the big bungalow in Segowlee cantonment. He was a mongoose, rather like a little cat in his fur and tail, but quite like a weasel in his head and his habits. His war cry as he scuttled through the long grass was Rikk-tikk-tikki-tikki-tchk!

One day a high summer flood washed him out of the burrow where he lived with his father and mother, and carried him, kicking and clucking down a roadside ditch. When he revived, he was lying in the hot sun on the middle of a garden path and a small boy was saying: "Here's a dead mongoose. Let's have a funeral!"

"No," said his mother, "let's take him in and dry him. Perhaps he isn't dead."

They took him into the house, and a big man picked him up between his finger and thumb and said he was not dead but half choked so they wrapped him in cotton wool, and warmed him over a little fire, and he opened his eyes and sneezed.

It's the hardest thing in the world to frighten a mongoose, because he is eaten up from nose to tail with curiosity. The motto of all the mongoose family is, "Run and find out," and Rikki-tikki was a true mongoose. He looked at the cotton wool, decided that it was not good to eat, ran all round the table, sat up and put his fur in order, scratched himself, and jumped on the small boy's shoulder.

"Don't be frightened Teddy," said his father. "That's his way of making friends. Let's give him something to eat."

They gave him a little piece of raw meat. Rikki-tikki liked it immensely, and when it was finished, he said to himself, "There are more things to find out about in this house than all my family could find out in all their lives. I shall certainly stay and find out."

He spent all that day roaming over the house. At nightfall, he ran into Teddy's nursery and when Teddy went to bed, Rikki-tikki climbed up too. "I don't like that," said Teddy's mother. "He may bite the child." "He'll do no such thing," said the father. "Teddy's safer with that little beast than if he had a bloodhound to watch him. If a snake came in the nursery now..."

questions.prompt: According to the author, Rikki-tikki-tavi most closely resembles which of the following animals?
choices: A) A bloodhound
B) A cat
C) A muskrat
D) A duck
E) A snake
questions.explanation: He is 'rather like a little cat in his fur and tail.' The weasel comparison isn't offered; among the choices, cat is the stated resemblance.

questions.prompt: Rikki-tikki-tavi decided to stay with the family because
choices: A) He liked the boy.
B) He was grateful that they saved his life.
C) He was interested in exploring the bungalow.
D) He did not know his way home.
E) His parents died in the flood that had carried him away.
questions.explanation: He reasons there are 'more things to find out about in this house...I shall certainly stay and find out'—curiosity about exploring, not gratitude or liking the boy.

questions.prompt: Rikki-tikki-tavi's name is apparently based on
choices: A) A sound that he makes
B) The place where he lives
C) His physical appearance
D) His will to survive
E) The motto, "run and find out"
questions.explanation: His war cry 'Rikk-tikk-tikki-tikki-tchk!' echoes his name, so it derives from a sound he makes, not appearance or the 'run and find out' motto.

questions.prompt: Based on the passage, each of the following words may be used to describe Rikki-tikki-tavi EXCEPT:
choices: A) Energetic
B) Brave
C) Curious
D) Clever
E) Eager
questions.explanation: Text shows energy (ran all round), bravery (hard to frighten), curiosity, and eagerness, but never demonstrates cleverness. 'Clever' is the unsupported trait.

passages.title: Florence Nightingale
passages.text:
Florence Nightingale was a woman ahead of her time. Before the nineteenth century, the profession of nursing was largely untrained. Midwives were the only practitioners who had any training at all. For the most part, sick people were looked after by the women of the house in their own homes.

Florence Nightingale began a school in London, England to set the standards for nursing. She was able to do this because she had already established a reputation for her work with soldiers during the Crimean War. She carried a lamp above her head as she walked among the wounded men, thereby earning the nickname "the lady with the lamp." It was this great lady who lit the way for nursing to become the respected profession it is today.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: A) the impact of nursing on the soldiers of the Crimean War
B) Florence Nightingale and her influence on the profession of nursing
C) the difference between nurses and midwives
D) how Florence Nightingale earned the nickname "the lady with the lamp"
E) why only females entered the profession of nursing
questions.explanation: The whole passage centers on Nightingale and how she 'lit the way for nursing to become the respected profession it is today'—her influence on nursing.

questions.prompt: Which of the following was a method most people used to receive care before Florence Nightingale's time?
choices: A) They would be cared for only by doctors.
B) They would be cared for by their children.
C) They were largely left uncared for.
D) They were cared for by midwives.
E) They were cared for by female relatives.
questions.explanation: 'Sick people were looked after by the women of the house in their own homes'—female relatives. Midwives were the only trained practitioners but not who cared for most people.

questions.prompt: The style of the passage is most like that found in a(n)
choices: A) personal letter to a trusted friend
B) anthology of short biographies of famous women
C) history of nineteenth-century England
D) textbook on medicine
E) editorial written for a daily paper
questions.explanation: A brief, admiring life-sketch of one notable woman reads like an entry in an anthology of short biographies, not a personal letter, medical textbook, or editorial.

questions.prompt: According to the author, the primary reason that Florence Nightingale was able to open a school for nursing was that
choices: A) she was already famous for her work in the war
B) her family was willing to finance her work
C) she had gained notoriety as a difficult woman to challenge
D) she had cared for many wealthy sick people herself
E) she worked endless hours every night
questions.explanation: 'She was able to do this because she had already established a reputation for her work with soldiers during the Crimean War'—she was already famous.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, all of the following could be said of nurses EXCEPT
choices: A) prior to Florence Nightingale, only midwives were trained
B) Florence Nightingale raised the standards of their profession
C) they are well-respected professionals today
D) they are exceedingly well paid for their work
E) prior to Florence Nightingale, their work was done often by female relatives
questions.explanation: Passage supports midwife-only training, raised standards, respect today, and prior care by female relatives. It never mentions pay, so 'exceedingly well paid' is unsupported.

passages.title: The Many Uses of Flax
passages.text:
Flax has been raised for many thousands of years, for many different reasons. Probably the two most important reasons are for the fabric made from it and the oil produced from it. The woody stem of the flax plant contains the long, strong fibers that are used to make linen. The seeds are rich in an oil important for its industrial uses.

The people of ancient Egypt, Assyria, and Mesopotamia raised flax for cloth; Egyptian mummies were wrapped in linen. Since the discovery of its drying ability, the oil from flaxseed, called linseed oil, has been used as a drying agent in paints and varnishes.

The best fiber and the best seed cannot be obtained from the same kinds of plant. Fiber flax grows tall and has few branches. It needs a short, cool growing season with plenty of rainfall evenly distributed. Otherwise, the plants become woody and the fiber is rough and dry. On the other hand, seed flax grows well in places that are too dry for fiber flax. The plants are lower to the ground and have more branches.

questions.prompt: Which of the following would be the best title for the passage?
choices: A) "How Mummies Were Preserved"
B) "The Many Uses of the Flax Plant"
C) "The Difference Between Seeds and Fibers"
D) "The Types of Plant Life Around the World"
E) "Ancient Sources of Oil and Linen"
questions.explanation: Passage surveys flax's fabric and oil uses plus how each type is grown—'The Many Uses of the Flax Plant.' 'Ancient Sources' ignores the modern/growing content.

questions.prompt: The author suggests that ancient people raised flax primarily for
choices: A) its oil, used to preserve wood
B) its oil, used as a rich source of nutrient
C) its fabric, used for their clothes
D) its fabric, used to wrap their dead
E) its fabric and oil, for industrial uses
questions.explanation: Ancient peoples 'raised flax for cloth,' i.e., fabric for clothing. Mummy-wrapping is one example of cloth use, not the stated primary purpose over clothing generally.

questions.prompt: This passage sounds as if it were an excerpt from
choices: A) a letter to the Egyptians
B) a book on plant life
C) a scientific treatise
D) a persuasive essay from an ecologist
E) a friendly reminder to a politician
questions.explanation: Neutral, informative description of a plant's uses and growth reads like a book on plant life, not a letter, persuasive essay, or reminder.

questions.prompt: Which of the following questions is answered by the passage?
choices: A) Can the same plant be grown for the best fabric and the best oil?
B) How did the Egyptians wrap their mummies?
C) What temperature is optimal for growing flax?
D) How is flax harvested?
E) Is it possible to produce a new type of flax for fabric and oil production?
questions.explanation: 'The best fiber and the best seed cannot be obtained from the same kinds of plant' answers whether one plant yields both best fabric and oil (no).

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the author most likely to discuss next?
choices: A) How flax is used around the world today
B) Other types of useful plants
C) Other sources of oil
D) The usefulness of synthetic fabrics
E) The advantages of pesticides and crop rotation
questions.explanation: Having covered flax's uses and growing types, a natural continuation stays on flax—how it's used around the world today—rather than jumping to other plants or oils.

passages.title: The Tower of London
passages.text:
William, Duke of Normandy, conquered England in 1066. One of the first tasks he undertook as king was the building of a fortress in the city of London. Begun in 1066 and completed several years later by William's son, William Rufus, this structure was called the White Tower.

The Tower of London is not just one building, but an 18-acre complex of buildings. In addition to the White Tower, there are 19 other towers. The Thames River flows by one side of the complex and a large moat, or shallow ditch, surrounds it. Once filled with water, the moat was drained in 1843 and is now covered with grass.

The Tower of London is the city's most popular tourist attraction. A great deal of fascinating history has taken place within its walls. The tower has served as a fortress, royal residence, prison, royal mint, public records office, observatory, military barracks, place of execution, and city zoo.

As recently as 1941, the tower was used as a prison for Adolf Hitler's associate Rudolf Hess. Although it is no longer used as a prison, the tower still houses the crown jewels and a great deal of English history.

questions.prompt: The primary purpose of this passage is to
choices: A) discuss the future of the Tower of London
B) discuss the ramifications of using the Tower as a prison
C) argue that the Tower is an improper place for crown jewels
D) describe and discuss the history of the Tower of London
E) debate the relative merits of the uses of the Tower in the past to the present
questions.explanation: Passage recounts the Tower's construction, structure, and its many historical roles. Trap E ('debate relative merits') is too argumentative; the piece is descriptive, not evaluative.

questions.prompt: All of the following were uses for the Tower of London EXCEPT
choices: A) a place where money was made
B) a palace for the royals
C) a place where executions were held
D) a place of religious pilgrimage
E) a place where records were stored
questions.explanation: Listed uses include fortress, residence, prison, mint, records office, observatory, barracks, execution, zoo. 'Religious pilgrimage' is never mentioned, so D is the exception.

questions.prompt: Which of the following questions is answered by the passage?
choices: A) What controversy has surrounded the Tower of London?
B) How much revenue does the Tower generate for England?
C) In what year did construction on the Tower of London begin?
D) What is the type of stone used in the Tower of London?
E) Who was the most famous prisoner in the Tower?
questions.explanation: Text states construction was 'begun in 1066.' Revenue, stone type, most famous prisoner, and controversy are never given, so only C is answerable.

questions.prompt: When discussing the Tower of London the author's tone could best be described as
choices: A) bewildered
B) objective
C) overly emotional
D) envious
E) disdainful
questions.explanation: The author reports facts and dates without emotional coloring, so 'objective' fits. No disdain, envy, or bewilderment is shown.

questions.prompt: Which of the following does the author imply about Rudolph Hess?
choices: A) He was executed at the Tower of London.
B) He was one of the last prisoners in the Tower of London.
C) He died an untimely death.
D) He was a tourist attraction.
E) He was respectful of the great Tower of London.
questions.explanation: Hess imprisoned 'as recently as 1941' and it is 'no longer used as a prison,' implying he was among the last prisoners. Nothing says he was executed or died.

passages.title: The Rise of the Automobile
passages.text:
The first old "horseless carriages" of the 1880s may have been worthy of a snicker or two, but not the cars of today. The progress that has been made over the last one hundred years has been phenomenal. In fact, much progress was made even in the first twenty years—in 1903, cars could travel at 70 miles per hour. The major change from the old cars to today is the expense. Whereas cars were once a luxury that only the very wealthy could afford, today, people of all income levels own cars.

In fact, there are so many cars that if they were to line up end to end, they would touch the moon. Cars are used for everyday transportation for millions of people, for recreation, and for work. Many people's jobs depend on cars—police officers, health care workers, and taxi drivers all rely on automobiles.

One thing that hasn't changed is how cars are powered. The first cars ran on gas and diesel fuel just as the most modern ones do. The newer cars, however, are much more fuel efficient and much research is devoted to saving fuel and finding new sources of energy for cars.

questions.prompt: The "progress" mentioned in line 2 most likely refers to
choices: A) the ability of a car to move forward
B) technological advancement
C) research
D) the new types of fuels available
E) the cost of the car
questions.explanation: 'Progress' pairs with cars going 70 mph by 1903, i.e., technological advancement. Cost (E) is introduced separately as 'the major change,' not the meaning of 'progress.'

questions.prompt: Which of the following is answered by the passage?
choices: A) What are some ways people use cars?
B) Why did people laugh at the "horseless carriage"?
C) Where will the fuels of the future come from?
D) When will cars become even more efficient?
E) How much money is spent on cars today?
questions.explanation: Passage lists cars used for transportation, recreation, and work, answering A. Future fuel sources, laughter reasons, and dollar amounts are not specified.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: A) the problem of fuel consumption
B) the difficulty of driving
C) the invention of the car
D) the development of the car from the past to now
E) the future of automobiles
questions.explanation: Passage traces cars from 1880s 'horseless carriages' to today. Trap E (future) is only the final point, not the whole focus.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, scientists devote much of their research today to
choices: A) making cars faster
B) making more cars
C) making cars more affordable
D) making cars more fuel efficient
E) making cars that hold more people
questions.explanation: Text: 'much research is devoted to saving fuel,' i.e., making cars more fuel efficient. Speed and affordability are not tied to current research.

questions.prompt: When discussing the technological advances of the early car, the author's tone could best be described as
choices: A) proud
B) hesitant
C) informative
D) pedantic
E) sarcastic
questions.explanation: Describing early advances (70 mph in 1903), the author simply presents facts admiringly but neutrally—informative. Not sarcastic or hesitant.

questions.prompt: The author would most likely agree that
choices: A) cars are incredibly useful to many different sorts of people
B) the problems we face in the future are very important
C) cars are more trouble than they are worth
D) early car owners were all snobs
E) we will never make the same technological advances as we did in the past
questions.explanation: Passage stresses many workers (police, health, taxi) rely on cars, supporting A. Negative options (trouble, snobs, never advance again) contradict the upbeat tone.

passages.title: The Concord Hymn
passages.text:
By the rude bridge that arched the flood,
Their flag to April's breeze unfurled,
Here once the embattled farmers stood
And fired the shot,heard round the world.
The foe long since in silence slept;
Alike the conqueror silent sleeps;
And Time the ruined bridge has swept
Down the dark stream which seaward creeps.
On this green bank, by this soft stream,
We set to-day a votive stone;
That memory may their deed redeem,
When, like our sires, our sons are gone.
Spirit, that made those heroes dare
To die, and leave their children free,
Bid Time and Nature gently spare
The shaft we raise to them and thee.

—"Concord Hymn" by Ralph Waldo Emerson

questions.prompt: The statements in lines 3-4 most likely mean
choices: A) the narrator is a farmer
B) the place described is a battle site
C) a crime took place at that site
D) the farmers described were all killed
E) it is a cold day
questions.explanation: 'Embattled farmers...fired the shot heard round the world' marks a battle site. Not a crime, not all killed, not about weather.

questions.prompt:
In the poem, the speaker claims which of the reasons for writing this poem?

I. To warn future generations about the horrors of war
II. To keep the memory of the great deeds of soldiers alive
III. To gain courage to fight himself
choices: A) I only
B) II only
C) II and III only
D) I and III only
E) I, II, and III
questions.explanation: 'That memory may their deed redeem' shows reason II (keep deeds alive). No warning of war's horrors (I) or seeking personal courage (III).

questions.prompt: The "votive stone" referred to in line 10 probably refers to
choices: A) a candle
B) a weapon
C) an old stone fence
D) a war memorial
E) a natural landmark
questions.explanation: 'We set to-day a votive stone; that memory may their deed redeem'—a commemorative shaft raised to the heroes, i.e., a war memorial, not a candle or fence.

questions.prompt: With which statement would the author most strongly agree?
choices: A) All war is in vain.
B) Farming is a difficult life.
C) It is important to remember the brave soldiers.
D) How a man fights is as important as how he lives his life.
E) A memorial is an insignificant way to remember the past.
questions.explanation: The whole hymn honors heroes so 'memory may their deed redeem'—it is important to remember brave soldiers. 'All war is vain' (A) contradicts the reverent tone.

passages.title: Carrots of Many Colors
passages.text:
Over two decades ago, scientists in the Agricultural Research Service Vegetable Crops Research Unit at Madison, Wisconsin, began a quest to breed carrots packed with beta-carotene—an orange pigment used by the body to create vitamin A. Thanks largely to their work, today's carrots provide consumers with 75 percent more beta-carotene than those available 25 years ago.

The researchers, led by plant geneticist Philipp Simon, haven't limited themselves to the color orange. They've selectively bred a rainbow of carrots—purple, red, yellow, even white. Scientists are learning that these plant pigments perform a range of protective duties in the human body.

Red carrots derive their color mainly from lycopene, a type of carotene believed to guard against heart disease and some cancers. Yellow carrots accumulate xanthophylls, pigments similar to beta-carotene that support good eye health. Purple carrots possess an entirely different class of pigments—anthocyanins—which act as powerful antioxidants.

While colored carrots are unusual, they're not exactly new. "Purple and yellow carrots were eaten more than 1,000 years ago in Afghanistan and 700 years ago in western Europe," says Simon. "But the carrot-breeding process has gone on intensively for just 50 years."

In nature, different strains of carrots contain varying types and amounts of carotenoids—the pigments responsible for orange, yellow, and red colors. To assist seed companies and growers who wish to produce nutrient-rich carrots, Simon and his lab are working to map all the genes that play a part in synthesizing carotenoids in major carrot lines. Simon now knows of 20 genes that are involved. But determining a particular gene's role in generating carotenoids is not that straightforward.

Aside from enhancing the nutritional value of carrots—as well as onions, garlic, and cucumbers—researchers at Simon's laboratory also work to improve the vegetable's culinary quality and appeal.

With their compelling health benefits and a thumbs-up from taste testers, Simon's colorful carrots will be a great addition to supermarket produce aisles once consumers create a demand for them.

questions.prompt: The main purpose of this passage is to
choices: A) express an opinion
B) analyze information
C) introduce characters and setting
D) resolve a crisis
E) set up a conflict between two characters
questions.explanation: Expository science article presenting research findings, so 'analyze information' fits best. Narrative options (characters, crisis, conflict) don't apply.

questions.prompt: The main purpose of this passage is to
choices: A) explain why carrots can be various colors
B) point out the health benefits of carotenoids
C) report on enhancements to the nutritional value of carrots
D) explore ways to increase carrot production
E) encourage people to buy carrots of various colors
questions.explanation: Passage centers on Simon's breeding work boosting carrots' beta-carotene and other nutrients—reporting nutritional enhancements. Health benefits (B) are supporting detail.

questions.prompt: The author's tone can best be described as
choices: A) argumentative
B) emotional
C) sentimental
D) informative
E) optimistic
questions.explanation: The author reports research facts neutrally—informative. 'Optimistic' (E) reflects only the closing line, not the whole passage; not argumentative or sentimental.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, carrots have been bred in all of the following colors EXCEPT:
choices: A) yellow
B) green
C) white
D) red
E) purple
questions.explanation: Carrots bred purple, red, yellow, white, and orange are named; green is never mentioned, so B is the exception.

questions.prompt: The type of carotene that gives carrots a red color is
choices: A) lycopene
B) xanthophyll
C) anthocyanin
D) beta-carotene
E) antioxidant
questions.explanation: Text: 'Red carrots derive their color mainly from lycopene.' Xanthophylls=yellow, anthocyanins=purple, beta-carotene=orange.

passages.title: Madam Walker Addresses the League
passages.text:
At the Thirteenth Annual Convention of the National Negro Business League in 1912, no women were included on the schedule of speakers. Madam C. J. Walker, a highly successful inventor and distributor of hair care products for African-American women, shocked the participants when she walked up and claimed the podium from moderator Booker T. Washington:

"Surely you are not going to shut the door in my face. I feel that I am in a business that is a credit to the womanhood of our race. I am a woman who started in business seven years ago with only $1.50. This year (up to the 19th day of this month ...) I had taken in $18,000. (Prolonged applause.) This makes a grand total of $63,049 made in my hair business in Indianapolis. (Applause.) I have been trying to get before you business people to tell you what I am doing. I am a woman that came from the cotton fields of the South; I was promoted from there to the wash-tub (laughter); then I was promoted to the cook kitchen, and from there I promoted myself into the business of manufacturing hair goods and preparations ... I am not ashamed of my past; I am not ashamed of my humble beginning. Don't think that because you have to go down in the wash-tub that you are any less a lady! (Prolonged applause.)"

questions.prompt: The purpose of Madam Walker's speech was to
choices: A) demonstrate her hair products to the league
B) lash out at Booker T. Washington for ignoring her
C) make the members of the league take notice of her business success
D) influence the members of the league to admit more women
E) encourage black women to go into business for themselves
questions.explanation: Excluded from speakers, she seizes the podium to recount her earnings and success: 'to tell you what I am doing.' The focus is her business achievement, not admitting women (D).

questions.prompt: As reflected in her speech, Madam Walker's attitude is best described as
choices: A) envious
B) proud
C) reverent
D) cautious
E) fearful
questions.explanation: 'I am not ashamed of my past...my humble beginning' and her boasting of profits convey pride. Not envious, fearful, or cautious.

questions.prompt: The reaction of the members of the league to Madam Walker's speech can be described as
choices: A) indifferent
B) angry
C) sad
D) favorable
E) critical
questions.explanation: Repeated '(Prolonged applause.)' and '(Applause.)' show a favorable, approving reaction, not anger or indifference.

passages.title: Mount St. Helens Awakens
passages.text:
On March 20, 1980, a series of small earthquakes signaled the awakening of Mount St. Helens from a 123-year slumber. Over the next two months more than 10,000 earthquakes followed as magma moved into the volcano, wedging the volcano apart at a rate of five feet per day. Heat from a rising plume of volcanic ash melted glacial ice creating cement-like slurries of rock and ash called mudflows. Superheated avalanches of hot gas, magma caused a visible swelling of the volcano's north flank creating a feature that scientists called "the bulge."

Many geologists weren't surprised by Mount St. Helens' awakening. In the 1950s, geologists had begun an extensive study of the deposits around Mount St. Helens. In 1975, they published a report predicting that Mount St. Helens was the volcano in the lower 48 states most likely to erupt by the end of the century.

On the morning of May 18, 1980, a magnitude 5.1 earthquake triggered the collapse of the summit and north flank of Mount St. Helens and formed the largest landslide in recorded history. Gas-rich magma and super-heated groundwater trapped inside the volcano were suddenly released in a powerful lateral blast. In less than three minutes, 230 square miles of forest lay flattened. The hot gas and magma melted the snow and ice that covered the volcano. The resulting floodwater mixed with the rock and debris to create concrete-like mudflows that scoured river valleys surrounding the mountain.

A plume of volcanic ash and pumice billowed out of the volcano reaching a height of 15 miles and transformed day into night across Eastern Washington. Avalanches of super-heated gas and pumice, called pyroclastic flows, swept down the flanks of the volcano. While the landslide and lateral blast were over within minutes, the eruption column, mudflows and pyroclastic flows continued throughout the day and following night. By the following morning major eruptive activity had ceased and the landscape appeared to be a gray wasteland.

questions.prompt: The primary purpose of this passage is to
choices: A) offer general information about volcanoes
B) describe a specific volcanic eruption
C) offer specific advice about what to do when a volcano erupts
D) provide historical background about volcanoes
E) appeal for action to protect the environment
questions.explanation: Passage narrates the 1980 eruption of one named volcano step by step, so B fits. A ('general information about volcanoes') fails because content is specific to Mount St. Helens, not volcanoes broadly.

questions.prompt: The first sentence implies that
choices: A) Mount St. Helens had never before erupted
B) Mount St. Helens erupts every 123 years
C) Mount St. Helens last erupted in 1857
D) no records exist for eruptions that occurred more than 123 years ago
E) no one suspected that Mount St. Helens could erupt
questions.explanation: Awakening in 1980 'from a 123-year slumber' implies the prior eruption was 1857 (1980−123), so C. A fails because 'slumber' implies earlier activity, not that it never erupted.

questions.prompt: As used in line 7, "rising" most nearly means
choices: A) growing
B) maturing
C) approaching
D) ascending
E) advancing
questions.explanation: A 'rising plume of volcanic ash' moves upward, so 'ascending' (D) fits. 'Growing' (A) describes size increase, not the upward motion the plume image conveys.
